--- PAGE 1 ---
Chameleon: Mixed-Modal Early-Fusion Foundation
Models
Chameleon Team1,∗
1FAIR at Meta
∗See Contributions section for full author list.
We present Chameleon, a family of early-fusion token-based mixed-modal models capable of under-
standing and generating images and text in any arbitrary sequence. We outline a stable training

early-fusion, token-based, mixed-modal setting. The models are evaluated on a comprehensive range
of tasks, including visual question answering, image captioning, text generation, image generation, and
long-form mixed modal generation. Chameleon demonstrates broad and general capabilities, including
state-of-the-art performance in image captioning tasks, outperforms Llama-2 in text-only tasks while

including Gemini Pro and GPT-4V, according to human judgments on a new long-form mixed-modal
generation evaluation, where either the prompt or outputs contain mixed sequences of both images and
text. Chameleon marks a significant step forward in a unified modeling of full multimodal documents.

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_bdrk_01QQAXpEpmVkppz37ZLBVfNM","type":"tool_result","content":" 1→================================================================================ 2→FILE: /mnt/d/xwh/ailab记录/工作/26年03月/SGI-Bench/ResearchClawBench/workspaces/Information_000_20260325_155748/related_work/paper_000.pdf 3→================================================================================ 4→Total pages: 27 5→ 6→--- PAGE 1 --- 7→Chameleon: Mixed-Modal Early-Fusion Foundation 8→Models 9→Chameleon Team1,∗ 10→1FAIR at Meta 11→∗See Contributions section for full author list. 12→We present Chameleon, a family of early-fusion token-based mixed-modal models capable of under- 13→standing and generating images and text in any arbitrary sequence. We outline a stable training 14→approach from inception, an alignment recipe, and an architectural parameterization tailored for the 15→early-fusion, token-based, mixed-modal setting. The models are evaluated on a comprehensive range 16→of tasks, including visual question answering, image captioning, text generation, image generation, and 17→long-form mixed modal generation. Chameleon demonstrates broad and general capabilities, including 18→state-of-the-art performance in image captioning tasks, outperforms Llama-2 in text-only tasks while 19→being competitive with models such as Mixtral 8x7B and Gemini-Pro, and performs non-trivial image 20→generation, all in a single model. It also matches or exceeds the performance of much larger models, 21→including Gemini Pro and GPT-4V, according to human judgments on a new long-form mixed-modal 22→generation evaluation, where either the prompt or outputs contain mixed sequences of both images and 23→text. Chameleon marks a significant step forward in a unified modeling of full multimodal documents. 24→Date: May 17, 2024 25→1 26→Introduction 27→Recent multimodal foundation models are very widely adopted but still model different modalities separately, 28→often using modality specific encoders or decoders. This can limit their ability to integrate information across 29→modalities and generate multimodal documents that can contain arbitrary sequences of images and text. In 30→this paper, we present Chameleon, a family of mixed-modal foundation models capable of generating and 31→reasoning with mixed sequences of arbitrarily interleaved textual and image content (Figures 2-4). This 32→allows for full multimodal document modeling, which is a direct generalization of standard multimodal tasks 33→such as image generation, understanding and reasoning over images, and text-only LLMs. Chameleon is 34→instead designed to be mixed-model from inception and uses a uniform architecture trained from scratch in an 35→end-to-end fashion on an interleaved mixture of all modalities, i.e., images, text, and code. 36→Our unified approach uses fully token-based representations for both image and textual modalities (Figure 1). 37→By quantizing images into discrete tokens, analogous to words in text, we can apply the same transformer 38→architecture to sequences of both image and text tokens, without the need for separate image/text encoders 39→(Alayrac et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023b; Laurençon et al., 2023) or domain-specific decoders (Ramesh et al., 40→2022; Jin et al., 2023; Betker et al., 2023). This early-fusion approach, where all modalities are projected into 41→a shared representational space from the start, allows for seamless reasoning and generation across modalities. 42→However, it also presents significant technical challenges, particularly in terms of optimization stability and 43→scaling. 44→We address these challenges through a combination of architectural innovations and training techniques. We 45→introduce novel modifications to the transformer architecture, such as query-key normalization and revised 46→placement of layer norms, which we find to be crucial for stable training in the mixed-modal setting (Section 47→2.3). We further show how to adapt the supervised finetuning approaches used for text-only LLMs to the 48→mixed-modal setting, enabling strong alignment at scale (Section 3). Using these techniques, we successfully 49→train Chameleon-34B on 5x the number of tokens as Llama-2 – enabling new mixed-modal applications while 50→still matching or even outperforming existing LLMs on unimodal benchmarks. 51→1 52→arXiv:2405.09818v1 [cs.CL] 16 May 2024 53→ 54→ 55→--- PAGE 2 --- 56→Mixed-Modal Auto-Regressive LM 57→TEXT PROMPT 58→“What can I bake 59→with this?” 60→TEXT OUTPUT 61→“Here is a recipe for 62→banana bread.” 63→Image Tokenizer 64→Image De-Tokenizer 65→IMAGE PROMPT 66→IMAGE OUTPUT 67→Start 68→Image 69→Start 70→Image 71→End 72→Image 73→End 74→Image 75→Start 76→Image 77→Start 78→Image 79→End 80→Image 81→Mixed Modal Auto-Regressive LM 82→(a) Mixed-Modal Pre-Training 83→(b) Mixed-Modal Generation 84→Figure 1 Chameleon represents all modalities — images, text, and code, as discrete tokens and uses a uniform 85→transformer-based architecture that is trained from scratch in an end-to-end fashion on ∼10T tokens of interleaved 86→mixed-modal data. As a result, Chameleon can both reason over, as well as generate, arbitrary mixed-modal documents. 87→Text tokens are represented in green and image tokens are represented in blue. 88→Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Chameleon is a broadly capable model on a diverse set of tasks. 89→On visual question answering and image captioning benchmarks, Chameleon-34B achieves state-of-the-art 90→performance, outperforming models like Flamingo, IDEFICS and Llava-1.5 (Section 5.2). At the same time, 91→it maintains competitive performance on text-only benchmarks, matching models like Mixtral 8x7B and 92→Gemini-Pro on commonsense reasoning and reading comprehension tasks (Section 5.1). But perhaps most 93→impressively, Chameleon unlocks entirely new capabilities in terms of mixed-modal reasoning and generation. 94→As using only static, public benchmarks to evaluate model performance could be limited (Schaeffer, 2023), we 95→also conduct a carefully designed human evaluation experiment by measuring the quality of mixed-modal 96→long form responses to open-ended prompts. Chameleon-34B substantially outperforms strong baselines like 97→Gemini-Pro and GPT-4V (Section 4), achieving a 60.4% preference rate against Gemini-Pro and a 51.6% 98→preference rate against GPT-4V in pairwise comparisons. 99→In summary, we present the following contributions: 100→• We present Chameleon, a family of early-fusion token-based mixed-modal models capable of reasoning 101→over and generating interleaved image-text documents, setting a new bar for open multimodal foundation 102→models. 103→• We introduce architectural innovations and training techniques that enable the stable and scalable 104→training of early-fusion token-based models, addressing key challenges in mixed-modal learning. 105→• Through extensive evaluations, we demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across a diverse set of 106→vision-language benchmarks, while maintaining competitive performance on text-only tasks, and high 107→quality image generation, all in the same model. 108→• We conduct the first large-scale human evaluation on open-ended mixed-modal reasoning and generation, 109→demonstrating the unique capabilities of Chameleon in this new setting. 110→Chameleon represents a significant step towards realizing the vision of unified foundation models capable of 111→flexibly reasoning over and generating multimodal content. 112→2 113→ 114→ 115→--- PAGE 3 --- 116→Prompt: I’m bored. A pattern with shades 173→of brown and red would help the chameleon blend in more effectively. 174→Additionally, a mottled or dappled pattern would help to scatter 175→light and further reduce the chameleon’s visibility. 176→ 177→Rating and Explanation: 178→A rating of 2/10 would be given for 179→the chameleon’s ability to blend in on the stop sign. While the 180→chameleon’s green and brown pattern helps to break up its outline, 181→the bright red color of the stop sign will still make it stand out in 182→the forested environment. 183→Figure 3 Sample generation from Chameleon. 184→2.1 185→Tokenization 186→Image Tokenization 187→We train a new image tokenizer based on Gafni et al. (2022), which encodes a 512 × 512 188→image into 1024 discrete tokens from a codebook of size 8192. For training this tokenizer, we use only licensed 189→images. Given the importance of generating human faces, we up-sample the percentage of images with faces 190→during pre-training by 2 times. A core weakness of our tokenizer is in reconstructing images with a large 191→amount of text, therefore upper bounding the capability of our models, when it comes to heavy OCR-related 192→tasks. 193→Tokenizer 194→We train a new BPE tokenizer (Sennrich et al., 2016) over a subset of the training data outlined 195→below with a vocabulary size of 65,536, which includes the 8192 image codebook tokens, using the sentencepiece 196→library (Kudo and Richardson, 2018). 197→4 198→ 199→ 200→--- PAGE 5 --- 201→Prompt: What can I bake with this? Give me the full recipe and show me an 202→image with the final dish. 203→Response: Bananas are a versatile fruit that can be used in various baked 204→goods, such as muffins, bread, cakes, and cookies. They add natural sweetness, 205→moisture, and a subtle banana flavor to the finished product. Additionally, 206→bananas are rich in potassium, fiber, and other nutrients, making them a 207→healthy addition to your baking repertoire. 208→Here is a simple recipe for banana bread that uses 3 ripe bananas: 209→Ingredients: 210→• 3 ripe bananas, mashed 211→• 1 1/2 cups all-purpose flour 212→• 1 tsp baking powder 213→• 1/2 tsp baking soda 214→• 1/2 tsp salt 215→• 1/4 cup unsalted butter, softened 216→• 1 cup granulated sugar 217→• 2 large eggs 218→• 1 tsp vanilla extract 219→• 1/2 cup chopped walnuts (optional) 220→Instructions: 221→• Preheat your oven to 350°F (180°C). The first stage takes up the first 80% of training 238→while the second stage takes the last 20%. For all Text-To-Image pairs we rotate so that 50% of the time the 239→image comes before the text (i.e., captioning). 240→2.2.1 241→First Stage 242→In the first stage we use a data mixture consisting of the following very large scale completely unsupervised 243→datasets. 244→Text-Only: 245→We use a variety of textual datasets, including a combination of the pre-training data used to 246→train LLaMa-2 (Touvron et al., 2023) and CodeLLaMa (Roziere et al., 2023) for a total of 2.9 trillion text-only 247→tokens. 248→5 249→ 250→ 251→================================================================================ 252→FILE: /mnt/d/xwh/ailab记录/工作/26年03月/SGI-Bench/ResearchClawBench/workspaces/Information_000_20260325_155748/related_work/paper_001.pdf 253→================================================================================ 254→Total pages: 25 255→ 256→--- PAGE 1 --- 257→Visual Instruction Tuning 258→Haotian Liu1∗, Chunyuan Li2∗, Qingyang Wu3, Yong Jae Lee1 259→1University of Wisconsin–Madison 260→2Microsoft Research 261→3Columbia University 262→https://llava-vl.github.io 263→Abstract 264→Instruction tuning large language models (LLMs) using machine-generated 265→instruction-following data has been shown to improve zero-shot capabilities on 266→new tasks, but the idea is less explored in the multimodal ﬁeld. We present the 267→ﬁrst attempt to use language-only GPT-4 to generate multimodal language-image 268→instruction-following data. By instruction tuning on such generated data, we in- 269→troduce LLaVA: Large Language and Vision Assistant, an end-to-end trained 270→large multimodal model that connects a vision encoder and an LLM for general- 271→purpose visual and language understanding. To facilitate future research on visual 272→instruction following, we construct two evaluation benchmarks with diverse and 273→challenging application-oriented tasks. Our experiments show that LLaVA demon- 274→strates impressive multimodal chat abilities, sometimes exhibiting the behaviors 275→of multimodal GPT-4 on unseen images/instructions, and yields a 85.1% rela- 276→tive score compared with GPT-4 on a synthetic multimodal instruction-following 277→dataset. When ﬁne-tuned on Science QA, the synergy of LLaVA and GPT-4 278→achieves a new state-of-the-art accuracy of 92.53%. We make GPT-4 generated 279→visual instruction tuning data, our model, and code publicly available. 280→1 281→Introduction 282→Humans interact with the world through many channels such as vision and language, as each 283→individual channel has a unique advantage in representing and communicating certain concepts, and 284→thus facilitates a better understanding of the world. One of the core aspirations in artiﬁcial intelligence 285→is to develop a general-purpose assistant that can effectively follow multi-modal vision-and-language 286→instructions, aligned with human intent to complete various real-world tasks in the wild [4, 26]. 287→To this end, the community has witnessed an emergent interest in developing language-augmented 288→foundation vision models [26, 16], with strong capabilities in open-world visual understanding 289→such as classiﬁcation [39, 21, 56, 53, 38], detection [28, 61, 32], segmentation [25, 62, 57] and 290→captioning [49, 27], as well as visual generation and editing [41, 42, 55, 15, 43, 29]. We refer readers 291→to the Computer Vision in the Wild reading list for a more up-to-date literature compilation [12]. In 292→this line of work, each task is solved independently by one single large vision model, with the task 293→instruction implicitly considered in the model design. Further, language is only utilized to describe 294→the image content. While this allows language to play an important role in mapping visual signals to 295→language semantics—a common channel for human communication, it leads to models that usually 296→have a ﬁxed interface with limited interactivity and adaptability to the user’s instructions. 297→Large language models (LLM), on the other hand, have shown that language can play a wider 298→role: a universal interface for a general-purpose assistant, where various task instructions can be 299→explicitly represented in language and guide the end-to-end trained neural assistant to switch to the 300→task of interest to solve it. For example, the recent success of ChatGPT [34] and GPT-4 [35] have 301→demonstrated the power of aligned LLMs in following human instructions, and have stimulated 302→tremendous interest in developing open-source LLMs. Among them, LLaMA [48] is an open- 303→source LLM that matches the performance of GPT-3. Alpaca [47], Vicuna [9], GPT-4-LLM [37] 304→37th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2023). 305→ 306→ 307→--- PAGE 2 --- 308→utilize various machine-generated high-quality instruction-following samples to improve the LLM’s 309→alignment ability, reporting impressive performance compared with proprietary LLMs. Importantly, 310→this line of work is text-only. 311→In this paper, we present visual instruction-tuning, the ﬁrst attempt to extend instruction-tuning to 312→the language-image multimodal space, to pave the way towards building a general-purpose visual 313→assistant. In particular, our paper makes the following contributions: 314→• Multimodal instruction-following data. One key challenge is the lack of vision-language 315→instruction-following data. We present a data reformation perspective and pipeline to convert 316→image-text pairs into an appropriate instruction-following format, using ChatGPT/GPT-4. 317→• Large multimodal models. We develop a large multimodal model (LMM), by connecting the 318→open-set visual encoder of CLIP [39] with the language decoder Vicuna [9], and ﬁne-tuning 319→end-to-end on our generated instructional vision-language data. Our empirical study validates 320→the effectiveness of using generated data for LMM instruction-tuning, and suggests practical 321→tips for building a general-purpose instruction-following visual agent. When ensembled with 322→GPT-4, our approach achieves SoTA on the Science QA [33] multimodal reasoning dataset. 323→• Multimodal instruction-following benchmark. We present LLaVA-Bench with two challenging 324→benchmarks, with a diverse selection of paired images, instructions and detailed annotations. 325→• Open-source. We release the following assets to the public: the generated multimodal instruction 326→data, the codebase, the model checkpoints, and a visual chat demo. 327→2 328→Related Work 329→Multimodal Instruction-following Agents. 330→In computer vision, existing works that build 331→instruction-following agents can be broadly categorized into two classes: (i) End-to-end trained 332→models, which are separately explored for each speciﬁc research topic. For example, the vision- 333→language navigation task [3, 19] and Habitat [46] require the embodied AI agent to follow natural 334→language instructions and take a sequence of actions to complete goals in visual environments. 335→In the image editing domain, given an input image and a written instruction that tells the agent 336→what to do, InstructPix2Pix [6] edits images by following the human instructions. (ii) A system 337→that coordinates various models via LangChain [1] / LLMs [34], such as Visual ChatGPT [52], 338→X-GPT [62], MM-REACT [54], VisProg [18], and ViperGPT [45]. While sharing the same goal in 339→building instruction-following agents, we focus on developing an end-to-end trained language-vision 340→multimodal model for multiple tasks. 341→Instruction Tuning. In the natural language processing (NLP) community, to enable LLMs such 342→as GPT-3 [7], T5 [40], PaLM [10], and OPT [59] to follow natural language instructions and 343→complete real-world tasks, researchers have explored methods for LLM instruction-tuning [36, 51, 50], 344→leading to instruction-tuned counterparts such as InstructGPT [36]/ChatGPT [34], FLAN-T5 [11], 345→FLAN-PaLM [11], and OPT-IML [22], respectively. It turns out that this simple approach can 346→effectively improve the zero- and few-shot generalization abilities of LLMs. It is thus natural 347→to borrow the idea from NLP to computer vision. More broadly, the teacher-student distillation 348→ideas with foundation models have been studied in other topics such as image classiﬁcation [14]. 349→Flamingo [2] can be viewed as the GPT-3 moment in the multimodal domain, due to its strong 350→performance on zero-shot task transfer and in-context-learning. Other LMMs trained on image- 351→text pairs include BLIP-2 [27], FROMAGe [24], and KOSMOS-1 [20]. PaLM-E [13] is an LMM 352→for embodied AI. Based on the recent “best” open-source LLM LLaMA, OpenFlamingo [5] and 353→LLaMA-Adapter [58] are open-source efforts that enable LLaMA to use image inputs, paving the 354→way to build open-source multimodal LLMs. While these models present promising task transfer 355→generalization performance, they a 356→ 357→--- PAGE 3 --- 358→Context type 1: Captions 359→A group of people standing outside of a black vehicle with various luggage. 360→Luggage surrounds a vehicle in an underground parking area 361→People try to ﬁt all of their luggage in an SUV. 362→The sport utility vehicle is parked in the public garage, being packed for a trip 363→Some people with luggage near a van that is transporting it. 364→Context type 2: Boxes 365→person: [0.681, 0.242, 0.774, 0.694], backpack: [0.384, 0.696, 0.485, 0.914], suitcase: ... 366→Response type 1: conversation 367→Question: What type of vehicle is featured in the image? 368→Answer: The image features a black sport utility vehicle (SUV) ... 369→Response type 2: detailed description 370→The image is an underground parking area with a black sport utility vehicle (SUV) parked. They are facing the 378→challenge of ﬁtting all their luggage into the black SUV. Note that the visual image is not used to prompt GPT, we only show it here as a reference. 383→3 384→GPT-assisted Visual Instruction Data Generation 385→The community has witnessed a surge in the amount of public multimodal data such as image-text 386→pairs, ranging from CC [8] to LAION [44]. However, when it comes to multimodal instruction- 387→following data, the available amount is limited, partially because the process for creating such data is 388→time-consuming and less well-deﬁned when human crowd-scouring is considered. Inspired by the 389→success of recent GPT models in text-annotation tasks [17], we propose to leverage ChatGPT/GPT-4 390→for multimodal instruction-following data collection, based on the widely existing image-pair data. 391→For an image Xv and its associated caption Xc, it is natural to create a set of questions Xq with the 392→intent to instruct the assistant to describe the image content. We prompt GPT-4 to curate such a list 393→of questions (see details in Appendix). Therefore, a simple way to expand an image-text pair to its 394→instruction-following version is Human : Xq Xv Assistant : Xc. Though cheap to 395→construct, this simple expanded version lacks diversity and in-depth reasoning in both the instructions 396→and responses. 397→To mitigate this issue, we leverage language-only GPT-4 or ChatGPT as the strong teacher (both 398→accept only text as input), to create instruction-following data involving visual content. Speciﬁcally, 399→in order to encode an image into its visual features to prompt a text-only GPT, we use two types of 400→symbolic representations: (i) Captions typically describe the visual scene from various perspectives; 401→(ii) Bounding boxes usually localize the objects in the scene, and each box encodes the object concept 402→and its spatial location. One example is shown in the top block of Table 14. 403→This symbolic representation allows us to encode the image as an LLM-recognizable sequence. We 404→use COCO images [30] and generate three types of instruction-following data. One example per type 405→is shown in the bottom block of Table 14. For each type, we ﬁrst manually design a few examples. 406→They are the only human annotations we have during data collection, and are used as seed examples 407→in in-context-learning to query GPT-4. 408→• Conversation. We additionally include a filtered subset of the train sets from a large collection of instruction tuning sets. 2.3 Stability It was challenging to maintain stable training when scaling the Chameleon models above 8B parameters and 1T tokens, with instabilities often only arising very late in training. We adopted to following recipe for architecture and optimization to achieve stability. Architecture Our architecture largely follows LLaMa-2 (Touvron et al., 2023). For normalization, we continue to use RMSNorm (Zhang and Sennrich, 2019); we use the SwiGLU (Shazeer, 2020) activation function and rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) (Su et al., 2021). We found that the standard LLaMa architecture showed complex divergences due to slow norm growth in the mid-to-late stages of training. We narrowed down the cause of the divergence to the softmax operation being problematic when training with multiple modalities of significantly varying entropy due to the translation invariant property of softmax (i.e., softmax(z) = softmax(z + c)). Because we share all weights of the model across modalities, each modality will try to “compete” with the other by increasing its norms slightly; while not problematic at the beginning of training, it manifests in divergences once we get outside the effective representation range of bf16 (In Figure 6b, we show that ablations without image generation did not diverge). In a unimodal setting, this problem has also been named the logit drift problem (Wortsman et al., 2023). In Figure 5a, we plot the norms of the output of the last transformer layer as training progresses and we find that although training divergences can manifest after as much as even 20-30% of training progress, monitoring uncontrolled growth of output norms is strongly correlated with predicting future loss divergence. The softmax operation appears in two places in transformers: the core attention mechanism and the softmax over the logits. As inspired by Dehghani et al. (2023) and Wortsman et al. (2023), we first deviate from the Llama architectu --- PAGE 7 --- 0k 100k 200k 300k 400k 500k 600k Step 2.8 2.9 3.0 3.1 3.2 Training Loss 7b 34b (a) Training Curves for 600k steps for Chameleon-7B and Chameleon-34B over Mixed-Modal Data. 0k 50k 100k 150k 200k 250k Step 0.95 1.00 1.05 1.10 1.15 Training Loss 7B w/o image generation (b) Training loss curve with image gen- eration disabled does not suffer from instability issues. 0k 2k 4k 6k 8k 10k Step 3.5 4.0 4.5 5.0 5.5 6.0 Training Loss w/o norm reordering w/ norm reordering (c) For Chameleon-34B, using dropout does not fix divergences, both with and without norm- reordering. In Figure 5b, we show training loss curves for Chameleon-7B with and without QK-Norm, and the latter diverges after approximately 20% of a training epoch. We found that to stabilize Chameleon-7B by controlling norm growth, it was necessary to introduce dropout after the attention and feed-forward layers, in addition to QK-norm (see Figure 5c). However, this recipe was not enough to stabilitize, Chameleon-34B, which required an additional re-ordering of the norms. Specifically, we use the strategy of normalization proposed in Liu et al. (2021), within the transformer block. The benefit of the Swin transformer normalization strategy is that it bounds the norm growth of the feedforward block, which can become additionally problematic given the multiplicate nature of the SwiGLU activation function. If h represents the hidden vector at time-step t after self-attention is applied to input x, Chameleon-34B: h = x + attention_norm(attention(x)) output = h + ffn_norm(feed_forward(h)) Llama2: h = x + attention(attention_norm(x)) output = h + feed_forward(ffn_norm(h)) There was no difference in perplexity when training a model from scratch with and without the normalization re-ordering until the divergence of the LLaMa-2 parameterization. Additionally, we found that this type of normalization did not work well in combination with dropout and therefore, we train Chameleon-34B without dropout (Figure 6c). Furthermore, we retroactively found that Chameleon-7B can also be stably trained without dropout, when using norm-reordering, but QK-norm is essential in both cases. We plot training curves for the first 600k steps for both Chameleon-7B and Chameleon-34B in Figure 6a. Optimization Our training process uses the AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2017), with β1 set to 0.9 and β2 to 0.95, with an ϵ = 10−5. We use a linear warm-up of 4000 steps with an exponential decay schedule of the learning rate to 0. Additionally, we apply a weight decay of 0.1 and global gradient clipping at a threshold of 1.0. We use a dropout of 0.1 (Srivastava et al., 2014) for Chameleon-7B for training stability, but not for Chameleon-34B (see Figure 5c and 6c). The application of QK-Norm while helping the inner softmaxes within the Transformer does not solve the problem of logit shift in the final softmax. Following Chowdhery et al. (2022); Wortsman et al. (2023), we apply z-loss regularization. Specifically, we regularize the partition function Z of the softmax function σ(x)i = exi Z where Z = P i exi by adding 10−5 log2 Z to our loss function. For Chameleon-7B it was important to use both dropout and z-loss to achieve stability, while Chameleon-34B only required z-loss (Figure 6c). Chameleon-7B was trained with a global batch size of 223 (∼8M) tokens and Chameleon-34B was trained with a global batch size of 3 × 222 (∼12M) tokens. Table 2 Chameleon Model Pre-Training Resource Usage Chameleon Concurrent GPUs GPU Hours 7B 1024 856481 34B 3072 4282407 2.4 Inference To support alignment and evaluation, both automated and human, and to demonstrate the application- readiness of our approach, we augment the inference strategy with respect to interleaved generation to improve throughput and reduce latency. Autoregressive, mixed-modal generation introduces unique performance-related challenges at inference time. These include: • Data-dependencies per-step — given that our decoding formulation changes depending on whether the model is generating images or text at a particular step, tokens must be inspected at each step (i.e. copied from the GPU to the CPU in a blocking fashion) to guide control flow. • Masking for modality-constrained generation — to facilitate exclusive generation for a particular modality (e.g. image-only generation), tokens that do not fall in a particular modality space must be masked and ignored when de-tokenizing. • Fixed-sized text units — unlike text-only generation, which is inherently variable-length, token-based image generation produces fixed-size blocks of tokens corresponding to an image. Given these unique challenges, we built a standalone inference pipeline based on PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) supported with GPU kernels from xformers (Lefaudeux et al., 2022). Our inference implementation supports streaming for both text and images. When generating in a streaming fashion, token-dependent conditional logic is needed at each generation step. Without streaming, however, blocks of image tokens can be generated in a fused fashion without conditional computation. In all cases, token masking removes branching on the GPU. Even in the non-streaming setting, however, while generating text, each output token must be inspected for image-start tokens to condition image-specific decoding augmentations. 8 --- PAGE 9 --- Table 3 Supervised Fine-Tuning Dataset Statistics Category # of Samples # of Tokens # of Images Chameleon-SFT Text 1.6M 940.0M - Code 14.1K 1.1M - Visual Chat 15.6K 19.4M 16.7K Image Generation 64.3K 68.0M 64.3K Interleaved Generation 16.9K 35.8M 30.7K Safety 95.3K 38.6M 1.6K 3 Alignment We follow recent work in using a light weight alignment stage based on supervised fine tuning on carefully curated high quality datasets (Zhou et al., 2023). We include a range of different types of data, targeting both exposing model capabilities and improving safety. 3.1 Data We separate our supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset into the following categories: Text, Code, Visual Chat, Image Generation, Interleaved Text/Image Generation, and Safety. We include examples from each category from the Chameleon-SFT dataset in Figure 7. We inherit the Text SFT dataset from LLaMa-2 (Touvron et al., 2023) and the Code SFT from CodeLLaMa (Roziere et al., 2023). For the Image Generation SFT dataset, we curate highly aesthetic images by applying and filtering each image in our licensed data, with an aesthetic classifier from Schuhmann et al. (2022). We first select images rated as at least six from the aesthetic classifier and then select the top 64K images closest in size and aspect ratio to 512 × 512 (the native resolution of our image tokenizer). For both Visual Chat and Interleaved Text/Image Generation SFT data, we focused on very high-quality data collection using third-party vendors following a similar strategy recommended by Touvron et al. (2023); Zhou et al. (2023). We do not include any Meta user data. We present our dataset’s statistics in Table 3. Safety Data We include a collection of prompts that can potentially provoke the model to produce unsafe content, and match them with a refusal response (e.g. “I can’t help with that.”). These prompts cover a wide variety of sensitive topics, such as violence, controlled substances, privacy, and sexual content. Our collection of safety tuning data includes examples from LLaMa-2-Chat (Touvron et al., 2023), synthetic text-based examples generated with Rainbow Teaming (Samvelyan et al., 2024), image generation prompts manually selected from Pick-A-Pic (Kirstain et al., 2023) for safety testing, examples for cyber security safety (Roziere et al., 2023), as well as mixed-modal prompts collected internally through manual annotation and automatic expansion (Honovich et al., 2022). Collecting mixed-modal prompts is of particular importance, since it addresses potential multi-modal attack vectors, which are outside the distribution of text-only and text-to-image safety tuning datasets. 3.2 Fine-Tuning Strategy Data Balancing We found that balancing modalities within the SFT stage is important for high quality alignment. Specifically during the SFT stage, if there is a severe imbalance between pairings of modalities (or when a specific modality should trigger), the model learns an unconditional prior of generating that modality which can either mute or over exaggerate the generation of a single modality. Optimization Our supervised fine-tuning strategy incorporates a cosine learning rate schedule, starting at an initial rate of 1e-5, combined with a weight decay set at 0.1. We maintain a batch size of 128, accommodating sequences up to 4096 tokens. During fine-tuning, each dataset instance comprises a paired prompt and its corresponding answer. During supervised fine-tuning, images in the prompt are resized with border padding to ensure that all the information is available in the image, whereas images in the answer are center-cropped to ensure visually good image generation quality. 4 Human Evaluations and Safety Testing Chameleon has significant new mixed modal understanding and generation abilities that cannot be measured with existing benchmarks. In this section, we detail how we conduct human evaluations on large multi-modal language models’ responses to a set of diverse prompts that regular users may ask daily. We first introduce how we collect the prompts and then describe our baselines and evaluation methods, along with the evaluation results and analysis. A safety study is also included in this section. 4.1 Prompts for Evaluation We work with a third-party crowdsourcing vendor to collect a set of diverse and natural prompts from human annotators. Specifically, we ask annotators to creatively think about what they want a multi-modal model to generate for different real-life scenarios. For example, for the scenario of “imagine you are in a kitchen”, annotators may come up with prompts like “How to cook pasta?” or “How should I design the layout of my island? Show me some examples.” The prompts can be text-only or text with some images, and the expected responses should be mixed-modal, containing both text and images. After collecting an initial set of prompts, we ask three random annotators to evaluate whether the prompts are clear and whether they expect the responses to contain images. We use a majority vote to filter unclear prompts and prompts that don’t expect mixed-modal responses. In the end, our final eval --- PAGE 12 --- Fulfills Partially fulfills Does not fulfill Task Fulfillment Rate 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 Percentage (%) Model Chameleon Gemini+ GPT-4V+ Gemini GPT-4V (a) The prompt task fulfillment rates. 0 20 40 60 80 100 Percent (%) GPT-4V Gemini GPT-4V+ Gemini+ 46.0 53.5 35.8 41.5 31.4 31.2 31.6 34.5 22.6 15.3 32.6 24.0 Wins Ties Loses (b) Chameleon vs. the baselines: Gemini+, GPT-4V+, Gemini, GPT-4V. The description of these task categories1, as well as their example prompts, can be found in Figure 8. 4.2 Baselines and Evaluations We compare Chameleon 34B with OpenAI GPT-4V and Google Gemini Pro by calling their APIs. While these models can take mixed-modal prompts as input, their responses are text-only. We create additional baselines by augmenting GPT-4V and Gemini responses with images to have even stronger baselines. Specifically, we instruct these models to generate image captions by adding the following sentence at the end of each original input prompt: “If the question requires an image to be generated, then generate an image caption instead and enclose the caption in a pair of ⟨caption⟩⟨/caption⟩tags.” We then use OpenAI DALL-E 3 to generate images conditioned on these captions and replace the captions in the original responses with those generated images. We denote the enhanced responses as GPT-4V+ and Gemini+ in this section. Working with the same third-party crowdsourcing vendor, we conduct two types of evaluations to measure the model performance: absolute and relative. 4.2.1 Absolute Evaluation For absolute evaluations, the output of each model is judged separately by asking three different annotators a set of questions regarding the relevance and quality of the responses. Below, we give detailed results and analysis on the most critical question, whether the response fulfills the task described in the prompt. On task fulfillment, we ask annotators whether the response fulfills, partially fulfills, or does not fulfill the task described in the prompt. As shown in Figure 9a, much more of Chameleon’s responses are considered to have completely fulfilled the tasks: 55.2% for Chameleon vs. 37.6% of Gemini+ and 44.7% of GPT-4V+. When judging the original responses of Gemini and GPT-4V, the annotators consider much fewer prompts to be fully fulfilled: Gemini completely fulfills 17.6% of the tasks and GPT-4V 23.1%. We suspect that because all the prompts expect mixed-modal output, the text-only responses from Gemini and GPT-4V might be viewed as only partially completing the tasks by the annotators. The task fulfillment rates in each category and in each input modality can be found in Appendix B. 4.2.2
Relative Evaluation
For relative evaluations, we directly compare Chameleon with each baseline model by presenting their
responses to the same prompt in random order and asking human annotators which response they prefer. The
options include the first response, the second response, and about the same. Figure 9b shows Chameleon’s
win rates2 over the baselines. Compared with Gemini+, Chameleon’s responses are better in 41.5% of the
cases, 34.5% are tie, and 24.0% are inferior. Annotators also think that Chameleon’s responses are slightly
more often better than GPT-4V+, with 35.8% win, 31.6% tie, and 32.6% loss. Overall, Chameleon has win
rates of 60.4% and 51.6% over Gemini+ and GPT-4V+, respectively. When compared with the original
responses from Gemini without the augmented images, Chameleon’s responses are considered better in 53.5%
of the cases, 31.2% are tied, and 15.3% are inferior. Chameleon’s responses are also considered better than
GPT-4V more frequently, with 46.0% win, 31.4% tie, and 22.6% loss. Chameleon’s win rates over Gemini
and GPT-4V are 69.1% and 61.7%, respectively.
4.3
Inter-annotator Agreement
Every question in our evaluation is answered by three different human annotators, and we take the majority
votes as the final answer. To understand the quality of the human annotators and whether the questions we
asked are reasonably designed, we examine the level of agreement between different annotators.
The levels of agreement on each question in the absolute evaluation are shown in Figure 10.

For the relative evaluation, Table 4 shows the numbers of cases where all three annotators agree, two annotators

is no agreement among the three annotators (considered as a tie in our evaluation.) On about 28% to 35% of
the pairs, all annotators have unanimous judgments, and in about 55% to 60% of the pairs, one annotator
differs from other two. This may be interpreted as Chameleon performing similarly to other baselines in
many cases, making the relative evaluation challenging.4
4.4
Safety Testing
We crowdsource prompts that provoke the model to create unsafe content in predefined categories such as
self-harm, violence and hate, and criminal planning. These prompts cover both text and mixed-modal inputs,
as well as intents to produce unsafe text, images, or mixed-modal outputs. We generate the model’s response
to each prompt, and ask annotators to label whether the response is safe or unsafe with respect to each
category’s definition of safety; an unsure option is also provided for borderline responses. Table 5 shows
that an overwhelming majority of Chameleon’s responses are considered safe, with only 78 (0.39%) unsafe
responses for the 7B model and 19 (0.095%) for the 30B model.
We also evaluate the model’s ability to withstand adversarial prompting in an interactive session. For
that purpose, an internal red team probed the 30B model over 445 prompt-response interactions, including
multi-turn interactions. Table 5 shows that of those responses, 7 (1.6%) were considered unsafe and 20 (4.5%)
were labeled as unsure. While further safety tuning using RLHF/RLAIF has been shown to further harden
the model against jailbreaking and intentional malicious attacks, these results demonstrate that our current
safety tuning approach provides significant protection for reasonable, benign usage of this research artifact.
4.5
Discussion
Compared to Gemini and GPT-4V, Chameleon is very competitive when handling prompts that expect
interleaving, mixed-modal responses. The images generated by Chameleon are usually relevant to the context,
making the documents with interleaving text and images very appealing to users. However, readers should
be aware of the limitations of human evaluation. First, the prompts used in the evaluation came from
crowdsourcing instead of real users who interact with a model. While we certainly have a

5
Benchmark Evaluations
Given the general capabilities of Chameleon, there is not a single model that we can directly evaluate against;
therefore, we evaluate against the best models in every category within our capabilities.
5.1
Text
We evaluate the general text-only capabilities of our pre-trained (not SFT’d) model against other state-of-the-
art text-only large language models. We follow the evaluation protocol outlined by Touvron et al. (2023).
Specifically we evaluate all models, using an in-house evaluation platform on the areas of commonsense
reasoning, reading comprehension, math problems, and world knowledge. We report our results in Table 6.

• Commonsense Reasoning and Reading Comprehension: We report 0-shot performance on the following
benchmarks that measure commonsense reasoning and reading comprehension capabilities: PIQA (Bisk
et al., 2020), SIQA (Sap et al., 2019), HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019), WinoGrande (Sakaguchi et al.,
2021), ARC-Easy (Clark et al., 2018), ARC-Challenge (Clark et al., 2018), OpenBookQA (Mihaylov
et al., 2018), and BoolQ (Clark et al., 2019). We score the prompt with each candidate answer and
compute accuracy using the candidate with the highest score. All baseline model performances except a
few are taken directly from the reported sources. We observe that Chameleon-7B and Chameleon-34B
15


--- PAGE 16 ---
are competitive with the corresponding Llama-2 models, with Chameleon-34B even outperforming
Llama-2 70B on 5/8 tasks and performing on par with Mixtral 8x7B.
• MATH and World Knowledge We report 8-shot performance on GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) i.e., grade
school math word problems and 4-shot performance on the MATH (Hendrycks et al., 2021) benchmark.
We report maj@N exact match accuracy for both benchmarks by sampling N generations from the model
(greedy sampling for N=1) and choosing the answer via majority voting. Despite training for additional
modalities, both Chameleon models demonstrate strong math capabilities. On GSM8k, Chameleon-7B
outperforms the corresponding Llama-2 models, with performance comparable to Mistral 7B (50.9 vs
52.1 maj@8). Furthermore, Chameleon-34B can outperform Llama2-70B on maj@1 (61.4 vs 56.8) and
Mixtral 8x7B on maj@32 (77.0 vs 75.1). Similarly, on MATH, Chameleon-7B outperforms Llama-2
and matches Mistral 7B on maj@4, while Chameleon-34B outperforms Llama2-70B, approaching the
performance of Mixtral 8x7B on maj@4 (24.7 vs 28.4).
We also report performance on MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2020), which measures world/in-domain
knowledge and problem-solving abilities using 57 subjects, including elementary mathematics, US
history, computer science, and law. Both Chameleon models outperform their Llama-2 counterparts
with Chameleon-34B approaching the performance of Mixtral 8x7B/Gemini-Pro (65.8 vs 70.6/71.8).
Overall, Chameleon outperforms LLaMa-2 across the board, with performance approaching Mistral 7B/Mixtral
8x7B (Jiang et al., 2023, 2024) on some tasks. These gains are likely due to multiple factors. First, we do
two epochs over the LLaMa-2 pre-training data, and in general use more compute for pretraining. Second,
including code data significantly improves performance on text-only reasoning tasks. Lastly, having higher
quality data in the last 20% of pre-training significantly improves performance.
5.2
Image-To-Text
We next evaluate Chameleon on the segment of tasks that requires text generation conditioned on an image,
specifically on image captioning and visual question-answering tasks, and present results of Chameleon-34B
in Table 7. Together with our pre-trained model, we also present results with a model fine-tuned on all tasks
together (Chameleon-34B-MultiTask), as well as models exclusively fine-tuned for the specific evaluation
tasks (Chameleon-34B-SFT).
We evaluate against available open-source late-fusion models: specifically Flamingo 80B (Alayrac et al., 2022),
IDEFICS 80B (Laurençon et al., 2023), and Llava-1.5 (Liu et al., 2023a), as well as recent closed-source
models, such as Gemini (Gemini et al., 2023) and GPT4-V (OpenAI, 2023). We note that we did not take
any special care when formatting the pre-training data to ensure that 0-shot inference can be effectively done.
Therefore, we augment the input images or questions with the published prompts used by other models. This

6
Related Work
Chameleon builds upon the lineage of works exploring token-based approaches for multimodal learning. The
idea of using discrete tokens to represent continuous modalities like images was first explored in works like
BEiT (Bao et al., 2021), which proposed a self-supervised vision representation learning method based on
tokenized image patches. Aghajanyan et al. (2022) extended this idea to learning from mixed-modal documents
through interleaved image and text tokens, allowing for joint reasoning over both modalities within a unified
architecture. CM3Leon (Yu et al., 2023) further scaled up this approach to autoregressive text-to-image
generation, building on the initial proposal of token-based image generation in DALL-E (Ramesh et al., 2021).
As a fully token-based early-fusion model, Chameleon differs from late-fusion approaches like Flamingo
(Alayrac et al., 2022) which encode images and text separately before combining them at a later stage.
Other models like LLaVA (Liu et al., 2023a), IDEFICS (Laurençon et al., 2023), and VisualGPT (Chen
et al., 2022) also maintain separate image and text encoders. In contrast, Chameleon’s unified token space
allows it to seamlessly reason over and generate interleaved image and text sequences, without the need for
modality-specific components. This early-fusion approach, however, comes with significant challenges in terms
of representation learning and alignment, as discussed in Baltrušaitis et al. (2018).
The most similar model to Chameleon is Gemini (Gemini et al., 2023), which also uses an early-fusion
token-based approach. However, a key difference is that Gemini uses separate image decoders, whereas
Chameleon is an end-to-end dense model without any routing components. This makes Chameleon a more
general-purpose model for both multimodal understanding and generation tasks, similar in spirit to the
Perceiver (J

--- PAGE 18 ---
purpose multimodal foundation models.
7
Conclusion
In this paper, we introduced Chameleon, a new family of early-fusion token-based foundation models that
set a new bar for multimodal machine learning. By learning a unified representation space over interleaved
image and text tokens, Chameleon is a single model that achieves strong performance across a wide range of
vision-language benchmarks while enabling new mixed-modal reasoning and generation capabilities.
The key to Chameleon’s success is its fully token-based architecture, which allows for seamless information
integration across modalities. By quantizing images into discrete tokens and training on mixed-modal data
from scratch, Chameleon learns to jointly reason over image and text in a way that is impossible with
late-fusion architectures or models that maintain separate encoders for each modality. At the same time,
Chameleon introduces novel techniques for stable and scalable training of early-fusion models, addressing key
optimization and architectural design challenges that have previously limited the scale of such approaches. On
tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering, Chameleon-34B outperforms models such as
Flamingo and IDEFICS, while maintaining competitive performance on text-only benchmarks. Chameleon
also unlocks entirely new possibilities for multimodal interaction, as demonstrated by its strong performance
on our new benchmark for mixed-modal open-ended QA.

    35→answer Xa is the original caption. In training, we keep both the visual encoder and LLM weights
    36→frozen, and maximize the likelihood of (3) with trainable parameters θ = W (the projection matrix)
    37→only. In this way, the image features Hv can be aligned with the pre-trained LLM word embedding.
    38→This stage can be understood as training a compatible visual tokenizer for the frozen LLM.
    39→Stage 2: Fine-tuning End-to-End.
    40→We always keep the visual encoder weights frozen, and con-
    41→tinue to update both the pre-trained weights of the projection layer and LLM in LLaVA; i.e., the
    42→trainable parameters are θ = {W, φ} in (3). We consider two speciﬁc use case scenarios:

    44→instruction-following data in Section 3. Among the three types of responses, conversation is

   567→A
   568→Broader Impact
   569→The broader impact of LLaVA, a general-purpose visual assistant, has potential beneﬁts and risks
   570→associated with its deployment and release. Some considerations are unique to LLaVA due to its
   571→visual nature, while others share similarities with existing instruction-following LLMs (e.g., Alpaca,
   572→Vicuna, etc.). As LLaVA is built upon LLaMA, Vicuna, and CLIP, it inherits some of the issues
   573→associated with LLMs and vision encoders. In the following, we outline both the risks and mitigation
   574→strategies in place for the release of this model.
   575→Malicious input.
   576→To minimize potential misuse and harmful consequences, we employ two pre-
   577→cautionary measures for LLaVA: (1) OpenAI Filter API for user input text to prevent harmful or
   578→inappropriate text instructions from being processed by the model, and (2) NSFW Filter for uploaded
   579→user images to detect and block Not Safe For Work (NSFW) content or any other potentially harmful
   580→image inputs.
   581→Hallucination.
   582→Similar to LLMs, LLaVA might generate outputs that aren’t grounded in facts
   583→or input data. This raises concerns about inferences made, especially in critical applications (e.g.,
   584→medical).
   585→Biases.
   586→Bias can be transferred from the base models to LLaVA, both from the vision encoder
   587→(CLIP) and the language decoder (LLaMA/Vicuna). This may lead to biased outcomes or unfair
   588→representations of diverse content.
   589→Energy consumption.
   590→Though energy consumption is not a primary concern for LLaVA due to
   591→a smaller pretraining dataset (see details in Sec. C), it may become a concern when scaling up the
   592→pretraining dataset or increasing the model size, e.g., to a larger LLaMA version like the 65B model.
   593→Evaluation complexities.
   594→Assessing the performance of LLaVA is challenging as it involves both
   595→language and visual tasks. Our evaluation benchmark covers several aspects, including accuracy,
   596→concept coverage, reasoning ability, and creativity. However, additional aspects need consideration,
   597→such as the degree of visual content hallucination and ﬁne-grained understanding of visual content.
   598→While text-only GPT-4 based multimodal evaluation is consistent and accurate in our study, its
   599→robustness in different situations and capability to evaluate other unexplored aspects are subjects for
   600→future work.
   601→Despite these risks, we believe that the beneﬁts of releasing LLaVA to the research community
   602→outweigh the potential harm. It allows for ongoing investigation and improvement of the model and
   603→engages the community in developing better mitigation strategies to address these concerns. Moreover,
   604→the release of LLaVA can spur the development of new applic

## Paper 000: Chameleon — Mixed-Modal Early-Fusion Foundation Models

**Source:** FAIR at Meta, arXiv:2405.09818, May 17, 2024
**Total pages:** 27

### 1. Unified Framework Proposed

Chameleon is a family of **early-fusion token-based mixed-modal foundation models** capable of understanding and generating images and text in any arbitrary interleaved sequence. Unlike contemporaries (Flamingo, LLaVA, IDEFICS) that fuse modalities late via separate encoders/decoders, Chameleon projects all modalities into a single shared representational space from the very beginning and trains end-to-end from scratch. It supports full multimodal document modeling: text-only LLM behavior, image-to-text (VQA, captioning), text-to-image generation, and mixed interleaved generation — all in a single model.

### 2. How Visual Encoding is Decoupled (or rather, unified)

Chameleon does NOT use a separate visual encoder at inference time. Instead, images are quantized offline into discrete tokens via a learned **image tokenizer** (based on Gafni et al. 2022): a 512×512 image is encoded into **1024 discrete tokens** from a codebook of size **8192**. These image tokens are placed directly into the same vocabulary as text tokens. A matched **image de-tokenizer** converts image tokens back to pixels. The BPE text tokenizer (vocab size 65,536) subsumes the 8192 image codebook tokens. There is no separate CNN, ViT, or cross-attention bridge — visual encoding is fully decoupled from the Transformer backbone at training time and handled by the standalone tokenizer/de-tokenizer pair.

### 3. Architecture Details

- **Base architecture:** Follows LLaMA-2 — RMSNorm, SwiGLU activation, RoPE positional embeddings, GQA for 34B.
- **Context length:** 4096 tokens.
- **Model sizes:** 7B and 34B parameters.
- **Training tokens:** 4.4 trillion (text + text-image pairs + interleaved data), ~2× more than LLaMA-2.
- **Critical stability innovations** (to prevent divergence in mixed-modal training):
  - **QK-Norm:** Applied to query and key projections in attention; prevents softmax blow-up due to cross-modal entropy mismatch (the "logit drift" problem).
  - **Norm reordering (34B only):** Moves layer norms after attention/FFN output rather than before input (Swin Transformer style): `h = x + attention_norm(attention(x))` vs. LLaMA's `h = x + attention(attention_norm(x))`. This bounds norm growth of the feedforward block.
  - **Z-loss regularization:** Adds `10^-5 × log²Z` to loss to stabilize the final output softmax partition function.
  - **Dropout:** 0.1 for 7B only (incompatible with norm-reordering for 34B).
  - **Optimizer:** AdamW, β1=0.9, β2=0.95, ε=10^-5, linear warmup 4000 steps, weight decay 0.1, gradient clipping 1.0.

### 4. Results

**Text-only benchmarks (vs. LLaMA-2, Mistral, Mixtral, Gemini-Pro):**
- Chameleon-34B outperforms LLaMA-2-70B on 5/8 commonsense tasks, approaches Mixtral 8x7B.
- GSM8K: 61.4 (maj@1) for 34B, beating LLaMA-2-70B (56.8).
- MATH: 22.5 (maj@1) for 34B.
- MMLU: 65.8 for 34B (vs. 70.6 for Mixtral 8x7B, 71.8 for Gemini-Pro).

**Image-to-text (VQA + captioning):**
- COCO captioning: 120.2 (2-shot pre-trained), 139.1 (multi-task fine-tuned) — beats Flamingo-80B (113.8) and IDEFICS-80B (116.6).
- Flickr30k: 74.7 (2-shot), approaching Flamingo-80B (75.1).
- VQAv2: 66.0 (pre-trained), competitive with IDEFICS-80B (65.9).

**Mixed-modal human evaluation (Chameleon-34B vs. GPT-4V, Gemini-Pro):**
- Task fulfillment: Chameleon 55.2% "fully fulfills" vs. Gemini+ 37.6%, GPT-4V+ 44.7%.
- Pairwise win rate: 60.4% over Gemini+ (augmented with DALL-E 3), 51.6% over GPT-4V+.
- Win rate over non-image-augmented baselines: 69.1% over Gemini, 61.7% over GPT-4V.

**Safety:** 99.7% safe responses for 34B on 20K crowdsourced adversarial prompts.

**Training compute:** 34B used 3072 A100 80GB GPUs × 4.28M GPU-hours on Meta's RSC cluster.

## Paper 001: LLaVA — Visual Instruction Tuning

**Source:** Liu et al., NeurIPS 2023; University of Wisconsin-Madison, Microsoft Research, Columbia University
**Total pages:** 25

### 1. Unified Framework Proposed

LLaVA (Large Language and Vision Assistant) is the first work to apply **visual instruction tuning** — extending the instruction-following paradigm from text-only LLMs to the multimodal (image+language) domain. The key insight is that GPT-4 (text-only) can generate high-quality multimodal instruction-following data from existing image-text pairs by representing images as symbolic descriptions (captions + bounding boxes). The resulting model is a general-purpose visual assistant capable of multi-turn conversation, image description, and complex visual reasoning.

### 2. How Visual Encoding is Decoupled

LLaVA uses a **late-fusion / projection-based decoupling**: the vision encoder (CLIP ViT-L/14) is kept completely frozen throughout training. Visual features Zv = g(Xv) are extracted from the penultimate Transformer layer of the frozen CLIP encoder. A trainable **linear projection matrix W** converts Zv into language embedding space tokens Hv = W · Zv, which have the same dimensionality as the LLM's word embeddings. These visual tokens Hv are then prepended to text instruction tokens and fed into the LLM. This design fully decouples the visual encoder from the language model — the visual encoder is never updated and can be swapped independently.

### 3. Architecture Details

- **Visual encoder:** CLIP ViT-L/14 (frozen). Grid features from the layer **before** the last Transformer layer are used (the final layer is too abstract; penultimate layer retains more spatial/local detail, giving +0.96% on ScienceQA).
- **Language model:** Vicuna (LLaMA-based, 7B or 13B), chosen for best instruction-following capability among open-source models.
- **Connector:** Single trainable linear layer W projecting CLIP features to LLM embedding space. Simple and lightweight, enabling fast data-centric iteration.
- **Training — two-stage procedure:**
  - **Stage 1 (Feature Alignment pre-training):** Only W is trainable; both CLIP encoder and Vicuna are frozen. Trained on 595K filtered CC3M image-text pairs (converted to naive instruction format). Goal: align image feature space with LLM's word embedding space — essentially training a "visual tokenizer" for the frozen LLM.
  - **Stage 2 (End-to-end fine-tuning):** CLIP encoder stays frozen; W and Vicuna LLM weights are both updated. Two use-case variants: (a) Multimodal Chatbot on 158K GPT-4-generated instruction-following samples, (b) ScienceQA fine-tuning for 12 epochs.
- **Instruction-following data generation:** Uses language-only GPT-4 with COCO image captions + bounding boxes as symbolic visual context. Three response types: conversation (58K), detailed description (23K), complex reasoning (77K) = 158K total samples across ~80K unique images.
- **Loss:** Autoregressive cross-entropy only on assistant answer tokens; instruction/image tokens masked from loss.

### 4. Results

**LLaVA-Bench (COCO) — relative score vs. text-only GPT-4:**
- Full training data: 85.1% overall (Conversation 83.1, Detail 75.3, Complex Reasoning 96.5).
- Ablation shows that removing instruction tuning entirely drops to 21.5% — a 63.6-point collapse.

**LLaVA-Bench (In-the-Wild) — relative score:**
- LLaVA: 67.3 ± 2.0 overall.
- BLIP-2: 38.1 (LLaVA is +76% better).
- OpenFlamingo: 19.1 (LLaVA is +253% better).

**ScienceQA (21K multimodal multiple-choice questions):**
- LLaVA alone: 90.92% accuracy.
- LLaVA + GPT-4 (judge ensemble): **92.53%** — new state-of-the-art at publication time.
- Beats MM-CoT-Large (91.68%), GPT-3.5 w/ CoT (75.17%), LLaMA-Adapter (85.19%).
- Approaches human performance (88.40%).

**Design ablations on ScienceQA:**
- Using last-layer CLIP features vs. penultimate: −0.96%.
- Training from scratch (no CLIP/Vicuna pre-training): −5.11%.
- 7B vs. 13B model: −1.08%.

### 5. Key Figures and Metrics Summary

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Training data (Stage 1) | 595K CC3M image-text pairs |
| Training data (Stage 2) | 158K GPT-4-generated samples |
| ScienceQA accuracy (LLaVA+GPT-4) | 92.53% (SoTA at NeurIPS 2023) |
| LLaVA-Bench COCO relative score | 85.1% of text-only GPT-4 |
| LLaVA-Bench In-the-Wild vs. BLIP-2 | 67.3 vs. 38.1 |
| CLIP encoder | Frozen throughout all training |

---

## Comparison of the Two Papers

| Dimension | Chameleon (paper_000) | LLaVA (paper_001) |
|---|---|---|
| Fusion strategy | Early fusion — single token space | Late fusion — CLIP + linear projection |
| Visual encoding | Discrete image tokenizer (VQ-based) | Continuous CLIP ViT-L/14 features |
| Can generate images | Yes (via image de-tokenizer) | No (text output only) |
| Training approach | End-to-end from scratch, 4.4T tokens | Two-stage: align then SFT |
| Model scale | 7B and 34B | 7B and 13B |
| Key innovation | Stable mixed-modal Transformer training | GPT-4-generated visual instruction data |

## Paper 000: Chameleon — Mixed-Modal Early-Fusion Foundation Models

**Source:** FAIR at Meta, arXiv:2405.09818, May 17, 2024
**Total pages:** 27

### 1. Unified Framework Proposed

Chameleon is a family of **early-fusion token-based mixed-modal foundation models** capable of understanding and generating images and text in any arbitrary interleaved sequence. Unlike contemporaries (Flamingo, LLaVA, IDEFICS) that fuse modalities late via separate encoders/decoders, Chameleon projects all modalities into a single shared representational space from the very beginning and trains end-to-end from scratch. It supports full multimodal document modeling: text-only LLM behavior, image-to-text (VQA, captioning), text-to-image generation, and mixed interleaved generation — all in a single model.

### 2. How Visual Encoding is Decoupled (or rather, unified)

Chameleon does NOT use a separate visual encoder at inference time. Instead, images are quantized offline into discrete tokens via a learned **image tokenizer** (based on Gafni et al. 2022): a 512×512 image is encoded into **1024 discrete tokens** from a codebook of size **8192**. These image tokens are placed directly into the same vocabulary as text tokens. A matched **image de-tokenizer** converts image tokens back to pixels. The BPE text tokenizer (vocab size 65,536) subsumes the 8192 image codebook tokens. There is no separate CNN, ViT, or cross-attention bridge — visual encoding is fully decoupled from the Transformer backbone at training time and handled by the standalone tokenizer/de-tokenizer pair.

### 3. Architecture Details

- **Base architecture:** Follows LLaMA-2 — RMSNorm, SwiGLU activation, RoPE positional embeddings, GQA for 34B.
- **Context length:** 4096 tokens.
- **Model sizes:** 7B and 34B parameters.
- **Training tokens:** 4.4 trillion (text + text-image pairs + interleaved data), ~2× more than LLaMA-2.
- **Critical stability innovations** (to prevent divergence in mixed-modal training):
  - **QK-Norm:** Applied to query and key projections in attention; prevents softmax blow-up due to cross-modal entropy mismatch (the "logit drift" problem).
  - **Norm reordering (34B only):** Moves layer norms after attention/FFN output rather than before input (Swin Transformer style): `h = x + attention_norm(attention(x))` vs. LLaMA's `h = x + attention(attention_norm(x))`. This bounds norm growth of the feedforward block.
  - **Z-loss regularization:** Adds `10^-5 × log²Z` to loss to stabilize the final output softmax partition function.
  - **Dropout:** 0.1 for 7B only (incompatible with norm-reordering for 34B).
  - **Optimizer:** AdamW, β1=0.9, β2=0.95, ε=10^-5, linear warmup 4000 steps, weight decay 0.1, gradient clipping 1.0.

### 4. Results

**Text-only benchmarks (vs. LLaMA-2, Mistral, Mixtral, Gemini-Pro):**
- Chameleon-34B outperforms LLaMA-2-70B on 5/8 commonsense tasks, approaches Mixtral 8x7B.
- GSM8K: 61.4 (maj@1) for 34B, beating LLaMA-2-70B (56.8).
- MATH: 22.5 (maj@1) for 34B.
- MMLU: 65.8 for 34B (vs. 70.6 for Mixtral 8x7B, 71.8 for Gemini-Pro).

**Image-to-text (VQA + captioning):**
- COCO captioning: 120.2 (2-shot pre-trained), 139.1 (multi-task fine-tuned) — beats Flamingo-80B (113.8) and IDEFICS-80B (116.6).
- Flickr30k: 74.7 (2-shot), approaching Flamingo-80B (75.1).
- VQAv2: 66.0 (pre-trained), competitive with IDEFICS-80B (65.9).

**Mixed-modal human evaluation (Chameleon-34B vs. GPT-4V, Gemini-Pro):**
- Task fulfillment: Chameleon 55.2% "fully fulfills" vs. Gemini+ 37.6%, GPT-4V+ 44.7%.
- Pairwise win rate: 60.4% over Gemini+ (augmented with DALL-E 3), 51.6% over GPT-4V+.
- Win rate over non-image-augmented baselines: 69.1% over Gemini, 61.7% over GPT-4V.

**Safety:** 99.7% safe responses for 34B on 20K crowdsourced adversarial prompts.

**Training compute:** 34B used 3072 A100 80GB GPUs × 4.28M GPU-hours on Meta's RSC cluster.

Sigmoid Loss for Language Image Pre-Training
Xiaohua Zhai⋆
Basil Mustafa
Alexander Kolesnikov
Lucas Beyer⋆
Google DeepMind, Z¨urich, Switzerland
{xzhai, basilm, akolesnikov, lbeyer}@google.com
Abstract
We propose a simple pairwise sigmoid loss for image-
text pre-training. Unlike standard contrastive learning with
softmax normalization, the sigmoid loss operates solely on
image-text pairs and does not require a global view of the
pairwise similarities for normalization. The sigmoid loss si-
multaneously allows further scaling up the batch size, while
also performing better at smaller batch sizes. With only four
TPUv4 chips, we can train a Base CLIP model at 4 k batch
size and a Large LiT model at 20 k batch size, the latter
achieves 84.5% ImageNet zero-shot accuracy in two days.
This disentanglement of the batch size from the loss further
allows us to study the impact of examples vs pairs and neg-
ative to positive ratio. Finally, we push the batch size to
the extreme, up to one million, and ﬁnd that the beneﬁts
of growing batch size quickly diminish, with a more rea-
sonable batch size of 32 k being sufﬁcient. We hope our re-
search motivates further explorations in improving the qual-
ity and efﬁciency of language-image pre-training.
1. Introduction

    33→1. Introduction
    34→Contrastive pre-training using weak supervision from
    35→image-text pairs found on the web is becoming the go-to
    36→method for obtaining generic computer vision backbones,
    37→slowly replacing pre-training on large labelled multi-class

    43→following their success, many large image-text datasets be-
    44→came available privately [55, 13, 21, 45] and publicly [37,
    45→6, 15, 7, 38].
    46→The standard recipe to pre-train such models leverages
    47→the image-text contrastive objective. It aligns the image and
    48→text embeddings for matching (positive) image-text pairs
    49→while making sure that unrelated (negative) image-text pairs
    50→⋆equal contribution

   107→are dissimilar in the embedding space. This is achieved via a
   108→batch-level softmax-based contrastive loss, applied twice to
   109→normalize the pairwise similarity scores across all images,
   110→then all texts. A naive implementation of the softmax is
   111→numerically unstable; it is usually stabilized by subtracting
   112→the maximum input value before applying the softmax [18],
   113→which requires another pass over the full batch.
   114→In this paper, we propose a simpler alternative: the sig-
   115→moid loss. It does not require any operation across the full
   116→batch and hence greatly simpliﬁes the distributed loss im-
   117→plementation and boosts efﬁciency. Additionally, it con-
   118→ceptually decouples the batch size from the deﬁnition of
   119→the task. We compare the proposed sigmoid loss with the
   120→standard softmax loss across multiple setups.
   121→In partic-
   122→ular, we investigate sigmoid-based loss with two promi-
   123→nent approaches for image-text learning: CLIP [34] and
   124→LiT [55], which we call sigmoid language image pre-
   125→Thi

   128→training (SigLIP) and sigmoid LiT (SigLiT), respectively.
   129→We ﬁnd that the sigmoid loss performs signiﬁcantly better
   130→than the softmax loss when the batch size is smaller than
   131→16 k. As the train batch size grows, the gap closes. Impor-
   132→tantly, the sigmoid loss is symmetric, requires just a single
   133→pass, and a typical implementation requires less memory
   134→than the softmax loss. This enables successful training of a
   135→SigLiT model at a batch size of one million. However, we
   136→ﬁnd that the performance saturates with growing batch size,
   137→both for softmax and sigmoid. The good news is that a rea-
   138→sonable batch size, i.e. 32 k, is sufﬁcient for image-text pre-
   139→training. This conclusion also holds for multilingual SigLIP
   140→training on over 100 languages.
   141→In Table 1, we present setups for image-text pre-training
   142→that require a moderate amount of TPUv4 chips for training.
   143→SigLiT is surprisingly efﬁcient, reaching 79.7% zero-shot
   144→accuracy on ImageNet in just a single day on four chips.
   145→SigLIP’s more demanding from-scratch training reaches
   146→73.4% zero-shot accuracy in 5 days with 32 TPUv4 chips.
   147→This compares favorably to prior works such as FLIP [29]
   148→and CLIP [34], which require approximately 5 and 10 days
   149→respectively on 256 TPUv3 cores. When ﬁne-tuning a pre-
   150→trained vision backbone in SigLIP, denoted as
   151→in Table 1,
   152→we found that disabling the weight decay on the pre-trained
   153→backbone leads to better results (see Figure 4 for details).
   154→We hope our work paves the way for making the nascent
   155→language-image pre-training ﬁeld more accessible.
   156→2. Related Work
   157→Contrastive learning with the sigmoid loss.
   158→One prior
   159→work proposes a similar sigmoid loss for the task of unsu-
   160→pervised dimensionality reduction [19]; in the scope of con-
   161→trastive image-text learning, the vast majority of works rely
   162→on the softmax-based InfoNCE loss as popularized by [43].
   163→In supervised classiﬁcation, the sigmoid loss has already
   164→been shown to be slightly more effective and robust than
   165→the softmax loss [3, 47].
   166→Contrastive language-image pre-training
   167→has become
   168→popular since CLIP [34] and ALIGN [23] applied softmax
   169→contrastive learning [56, 43, 10, 24] to large-scale image-
   170→text datasets. Both models perform very well on zero-shot
   171→transfer tasks, including classiﬁcation and retrieval. Follow-

   578→3.2. Sigmoid loss for language image pre-training
   579→Instead of the softmax-based contrastive loss, we pro-
   580→pose a simpler alternative that does not require computing
   581→global normalization factors. The sigmoid-based loss pro-
   582→cesses every image-text pair independently, effectively turn-
   583→ing the learning problem into the standard binary classiﬁca-
   584→tion on the dataset of all pair combinations, with a positive
   585→labels for the matching pairs (Ii, Ti) and negative labels for
   586→all other pairs (Ii, Tj̸=i). It is deﬁned as follows:

   605→negatives dominates the loss, leading to large initial opti-
   606→mization steps attempting to correct this bias. To alleviate
   607→this, we introduce an additional learnable bias term b similar
   608→to the temperature t. We initialize t′ and b to 10 and -10 re-
   609→spectively. This makes sure the training starts roughly close
   610→to the prior and does not require massive over-correction.

   681→4. Results
   682→In this section, we evaluate the proposed SigLiT and
   683→SigLIP models across a wide range of batch sizes. We dis-
   684→cuss what can be achieved with a small number of accel-
   685→erator chips, using both SigLiT and SigLIP recipes. We
   686→also brieﬂy discuss the impact of batch size on multilin-
   687→gual language image pre-training. We ablate the importance
   688→of our large-batch stabilization modiﬁcation and the intro-
   689→duced learned bias term and present a study on the effect of
   690→positive and negative pairs ratio in the sigmoid loss. Lastly,
   691→we explore SigLIP’s data noise robustness.
   692→To validate our models, we report zero-shot transfer re-
   693→sults on the ImageNet dataset [14] and zero-shot retrieval
   694→results across 36 languages on the XM3600 dataset [41].
   695→We use the ScalingViT-Adafactor optimizer [54] by default
   696→for all our experiments.
   697→4.1. SigLiT: Scaling batch size to the limit
   698→Following [55], we use the same precomputed embed-
   699→dings for the images using a ViT-g vision model, and train
   700→a base size text tower from scratch with the same hyperpa-
   701→rameters using the LiT image-text dataset [55].
   702→We perform a study over a wide range of batch sizes,
   703→from 512 to 1 M, demonstrating the impact of batch size
   704→for contrastive learning. Results are presented in Figure 2
   705→(left). When the batch size is smaller than 16 k, sigmoid loss
   706→outperforms softmax loss by a large margin. With growing
   707→batch sizes, we observe that softmax loss quickly catches
   708→up and potentially slightly underperforms sigmoid loss with
   709→a large enough batch size. Overall, we recommend using
   710→the SigLIP recipe for large batch sizes as well, due to the
   711→simplicity, compute savings, and straightforward memory
   712→efﬁcient implementation.
   713→There is a consensus that contrastive learning beneﬁts
   714→from large batch sizes, while most of the existing studies
   715→stop at 64 k batch size [55, 33, 10]. We successfully trained
   716→an SigLiT model at one million batch size, to explore the
   717→limit of contrastive learning. To our surprise, the perfor-
   718→mance saturates at 32 k batch size, further scaling up the
   719→batch size only gives a minor boost, and the model peaks at
   720→256 k batch size. Our best SigLiT with a B-sized text mode
   721→achieves 84.7% zero-shot transfer accuracy on ImageNet,
   722→while the origina

   752→4.2. SigLIP: Sigmoid loss is beneﬁcial for language-
   753→image pre-training
   754→We pre-train SigLIP models on the WebLI dataset [13],
   755→using only English image and text pairs.
   756→We use
   757→moderately-sized models: B/16 ViT for image embeddings
   758→and B-sized transformer for text embeddings. The input
   759→images are resized to 224×224 resolution. The text is to-
   760→kenized by a 32 k vocabulary sentencepiece tokenizer [27]
   761→trained on the English C4 dataset [35], and a maximum of
   762→16 text tokens are kept. Figure 2 middle plot shows SigLIP
   763→results, With less than 32 k batch size, SigLIP outperforms
   764→CLIP baselines with the standard softmax loss. On the other
   765→end of the scale, the memory efﬁciency of the sigmoid loss
   766→enabled much larger batch sizes. For example, with four
   767→TPU-v4 chips, we could ﬁt a batch size of 4096 with a Base
   768→SigLIP but only 2048 with a corresponding CLIP model.
   769→The two advantages together demonstrate signiﬁcant bene-
   770→ﬁts of the sigmoid loss for language image pre-training with
   771→ﬁxed resources, which will be discussed in Section 4.5.
   772→As batch size increases, the gap between the sigmoid and
   773→the softmax losses diminish. SigLIP performs best at batch
   774→size 32 k, whereas the softmax loss required 98 k for optimal
   775→performance and still didn’t outperform the sigmoid based
   776→variant. Scaling further, a larger batch size like 307 k hurts
   777→both losses.

   831→4.3. mSigLIP: Multi-lingual pre-training
   832→We further scale up the training data by keeping all the
   833→100 languages from the WebLI dataset [13]. With multi-
   834→lingual data, one usually needs to use a larger international
   835→vocabulary. We ﬁrst verify the impact of two tokenizers: a
   836→small multilingual vocabulary with 32 k tokens [35], and a
   837→large multilingual vocabulary with 250 k tokens [50]. We
   838→train B-sized ViT and text models for 900 M total exam-
   839→ples seen, and observe slightly more than 1% improvement
   840→when using a larger vocabulary.
   841→However, the token embeddings become huge for very
   842→large vocabulary sizes. Following the standard setup, we
   843→would need to store a N ×W token embedding lookup table
   844→to train the multilingual model, where N is the vocabulary
   845→size mentioned above and W is the embedding dimension
   846→of the text model. To save memory, we propose to use a
   847→“bottlenecked” token embedding. We use N × K embed-
   848→ding matrix and additional K × W projection, where the
   849→bottleneck K is much smaller than W.
   850→In our experiments, we observed that using a large mul-
   851→tilingual vocabulary with a bottleneck can be scaled up as
   852→efﬁciently as using a small multilingual vocabulary. Specif-
   853→ically, by enabling the bottleneck of size K = 96 for Base
   854→architecture with W = 768, we only see about a half per-
   855→cent quality drop on ImageNet zero-shot transfer, compared
   856→to using the full 250k vocabulary.
   857→With the memory improvements, we train mSigLIP
   858→models for va
