   954→cay to 0. We train for a total of 65 000 steps with a batch
   955→size of 32k – this leads to just under one day of training.
   956→Table 1 shows the results when training a model on four
   957→chips for one day, achieving 79.7% 0-shot ImageNet classi-
   958→ﬁcation accuracy; very competitive in this limited resource
   959→regime. With a ViT-g/14 [54] model as the vision tower and
   960→a Large text tower, we can train at 20 k batch size on four
   961→chips for 107 k steps in under two days. This further pushes
   962→the 0-shot ImageNet classiﬁcation accuracy up to 84.5%.
   963→4.5. SigLIP with a small amount of TPU-v4 chips
   964→It’s resource demanding to train a CLIP model from-
   965→scratch in general, with SigLIP it’s possible to ﬁt a larger
   966→train batch size with fewer amount of chips. In this section,
   967→we explore ways to train SigLIP models efﬁciently with pre-
   968→trained weights. We use pre-trained weights to initialize the
   969→image model to accelerate the pre-training, which was orig-
   970→inally discussed in [55]. We use the public and unlocked
   971→ViT-Augreg-B/16 [39] model to initialize our vision tower
   972→and ﬁne-tune on the same WebLI English data as used for
   973→SigLIP. In all the experiments, we apply a 0.1 learning rate
   974→multiplier to the pre-trained image tower to make it suitable

  1031→We hypothesize that the default weight decay applied to
  1032→the pre-trained weights reduces their effectiveness. Moti-
  1033→vated by the ﬁne-tuning recipe from [17, 54, 25], that uses
  1034→no weight decay, we also propose disabling weight decay on
  1035→the pre-trained weights for SigLIP training. Weight decay
  1036→is therefore only applied to the randomly initialized weights
  1037→in the text model. This simple modiﬁcation signiﬁcantly
  1038→improved SigLIP results. Figure 4 shows that with our im-
  1039→proved recipe, SigLIP reaches 71% 0-shot accuracy on Im-
  1040→ageNet, using 16k batch size, trained on 16 chips for three
  1041→days. We also present from-scratch results in the bottom
  1042→rows of Table 1: with 32 TPUv4 chips for only two days,
  1043→SigLIP achieves 72.1% 0-shot accuracy. This presents a
  1044→signiﬁcant training cost reduction e.g. compared to CLIP
  1045→(approx. 2500 TPUv3-days for 72.6%) reported in [29].
  1046→4.6. Scaling up SigLIP
  1047→In this section, we scale up SigLIP by “overtraining”
  1048→the model [42, 1] for a long schedule. We present results
  1049→in Table 3 using ViT-B or ViT-L as the vision encoder,
  1050→while the text encoder is kept the same size (B or L respec-
  1051→tively). Following the recipe as described in Section 4.2,
  1052→we train both models for 40 billion examples seen, but in-
  1053→crease the number of image patches from (224/16)2 = 196
  1054→to (256/16)2 = 256 and the number of text tokens from
  1055→16 to 64. To get SigLIP results for different resolutions,
  1056→we continue training the pre-trained model for 5 billion
  1057→examples on the target resolutions (i.e. 224 or 384), us-
  1058→ing a 100x smaller learning rate without weight decay. In
  1059→Table 3, we report zero-shot classiﬁcation results on Im-
  1060→ageNet [14], ObjectNet [2], ImageNetv2 [36], ImageNet
  1061→ReaL [3], and zero-shot image-to-text (I→T) retrieval, text-
  1062→to-image (I→T) retrieval results on MSCOCO [11]. Our
  1063→SigLIP models outperform the previous models consistently
  1064→with ViT-B and ViT-L architectures.
  1065→4.7. Stabilizing large-batch training
  1066→As we move to large batch sizes, the language image pre-
  1067→training using transformers becomes increasingly more un-
  1068→stable, even when using a modestly-sized model (e.g. Base
  1069→size). The reason for these instabilities is large spikes in the
  1070→gradient norms, which translate to large-magnitude changes
  1071→in the weights that may destabilize the training process,
  1072→see Figure 5. We observe that reducing β2 in Adam and
  1073→AdaFactor from its default 0.999 to 0.95 (which was sug-
  1074→gested in [20, 9]) is enough to stabilize the training. Intu-
  1075→itively, this allows recovering from gradient spikes quicker.
  1076→We opt for setting β2 = 0.95 for all

  1192→the recently introduced EVA-CLIP [40] model, SigLIP-L outperforms EVA-CLIP-L on all the tasks with the same number of

  1194→moid’s “rate this pair” view, is the imbalance in positive
  1195→versus negative pairs. For a batch size |B|, the batch con-
  1196→tains |B| positive pairs, but |B|2 −|B| negative examples.
  1197→In the modest batch-size of 16 k, there are actually 268 M
  1198→negative examples for only 16 k positive ones. At the same
  1199→time, because the sigmoid loss decomposes into a sum of
  1200→per-example losses, we can perform controlled experiments
  1201→to study the effect of the mini-batch composition and dis-
  1202→tribution of examples visited. We run experiments in the
  1203→SigLiT setup at batch-size 16 k for 900 M steps and vary
  1204→the composition of the batch by masking out (i.e. ignoring)
  1205→enough negative examples to reach a target “positive : neg-
  1206→ative” ratio, masking in the following ways:
  1207→• Random: Randomly choose negative pairs to mask.
  1208→• Hard: Keep hardest negative pairs (highest loss).
  1209→• Easy: Keep easiest negatives pairs (lowest loss).
  1210→• Hard + matching total pairs seen: Masking exam-
  1211→ples while training for a ﬁxed number of steps does
  1212→decrease the total number of pairs seen during train-
  1213→ing. Hence in the matched pairs setting, we increase
  1214→the number of training steps by the masking ratio in
  1215→order to keep the number of pairs seen constant.
  1216→Figure 6 shows the effect of the various masking strate-
  1217→gies. Randomly removing negatives to rebalance does dete-
  1218→riorate performance. Keeping the easiest examples does not
  1219→work at all, while keeping the hardest negatives does almost
  1220→maintain the quality, indicating that, as could be expected,
  1221→a lot of the learning on the negative side comes from the
  1222→harder examples. This is further conﬁrmed by the slightly
  1223→increased performance of training longer on the hardest ex-
  1224→amples in order to match the total pairs seen.
  1225→We also look at the value of the learned bias at the end of
  1226→training as well as the average logit value for positive and
  1227→negative examples across these settings, and ﬁnd the result
  1228→mostly follows what one would expect: as fewer negatives
  1229→are present, the bias and logits become more positive over-
  1230→all. Interestingly, when training with more hard negative
  1231→pairs, the average logits of positive pairs stays mostly ﬂat.
  1232→This study conﬁrms that (1) the imbalance does not seem
  1233→to be a major reason for concern, while at the same time (2)
  1234→coming up with an efﬁcient way of including more negative
  1235→examples can be promising but is not trivial.

  1278→Autoregressive Model Beats Diffusion: Llama for
  1279→Scalable Image Generation
  1280→Peize Sun1
  1281→Yi Jiang2†
  1282→Shoufa Chen1
  1283→Shilong Zhang1
  1284→Bingyue Peng2
  1285→Ping Luo1∗
  1286→Zehuan Yuan2∗
  1287→1The University of Hong Kong
  1288→2ByteDance
  1289→Codes and models: https://github.com/FoundationVision/LlamaGen
  1290→Figure 1: Image generation with vanilla autoregressive models. We show samples from our
  1291→class-conditional image (top row) and text-conditional image (bottom row) generation models.
  1292→Abstract
  1293→We introduce LlamaGen, a new family of image generation models that apply origi-
  1294→nal “next-token prediction” paradigm of large language models to visual generation
  1295→domain. It is an affirmative answer to whether vanilla autoregressive models, e.g.,
  1296→Llama, without inductive biases on visual signals can achieve state-of-the-art image
  1297→generation performance if scaling properly. We reexamine design spaces of image
  1298→tokenizers, scalability properties of image generation models, and their training
  1299→data quality. The outcome of this exploration consists of: (1) An image tokenizer
  1300→with downsample ratio of 16, reconstruction quality of 0.94 rFID and codebook
  1301→usage of 97% on ImageNet benchmark. (2) A series of class-conditional image
  1302→generation models ranging from 111M to 3.1B parameters, achieving 2.18 FID on
  1303→ImageNet 256×256 benchmarks, outperforming the popular diffusion models such
  1304→as LDM, DiT. (3) A text-conditional image generation model with 775M parame-
  1305→ters, from two-stage training on LAION-COCO and high aesthetics quality images,
  1306→demonstrating competitive performance of visual quality and text alignment. (4)
  1307→We verify the effectiveness of LLM serving frameworks in optimizing the inference
  1308→speed of image generation models and achieve 326% - 414% speedup. We release
  1309→all models and codes to facilitate open-source community of visual generation and
  1310→multimodal foundation models.
  1311→∗: Corresponding authors, †: project lead
  1312→arXiv:2406.06525v1  [cs.CV]  10 Jun 2024

  1316→1
  1317→Introduction
  1318→Built upon autoregressive models, large language models (LLMs) [Vaswani et al. 2017; Devlin et al.
  1319→2018; Radford et al. 2018; Raffel et al. 2020; Radford et al. 2019; Brown et al. 2020; Zhang et al.
  1320→2022] generate the text by predicting the next token in a sequence. This “next-token prediction”
  1321→paradigm presents unprecedented capabilities in solving language tasks in a human-like conversational
  1322→manner [Ouyang et al. 2022; OpenAI 2022, 2023b; Google 2023; Anthropic 2023; Workshop et al.
  1323→2022; Touvron et al. 2023a,b; Bai et al. 2023a; Yang et al. 2023; Team 2023; Bi et al. 2024] and
  1324→incredible scalability [Kaplan et al. 2020; Henighan et al. 2020; Hoffmann et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022;
  1325→Alabdulmohsin et al. 2022; Chowdhery et al. 2023; Anil et al. 2023], demonstrating a promising path
  1326→toward general-purpose artificial intelligence models.
  1327→Witnessed the scalability of autoregressive models on large language models, pioneering works
  1328→attempt to explore autoregressive models in image generation, for example, VQVAE [Van Den Oord
  1329→et al. 2017; Razavi et al. 2019], VQGAN [Esser et al. 2021; Lee et al. 2022], DALL-E [Ramesh et al.
  1330→2021], Parti [Yu et al. 2021, 2022]. They introduce image tokenizers to convert continuous images to
  1331→discrete tokens, and apply autoregressive models to generate image tokens in the way of next-token
  1332→prediction. They demonstrate strong performance among their contemporaries [Brock et al. 2018;
  1333→Ho et al. 2020; Dhariwal & Nichol 2021] in the year before 2022. However, their open-source
  1334→communities are not well developed, which largely limits their further improvements.
  1335→At the same period, another image generation method, diffusion models [Song & Ermon 2019; Ho
  1336→et al. 2020; Song et al. 2020; Dhariwal & Nichol 2021; Nichol et al. 2021; Lu et al. 2022a; Ho
  1337→et al. 2022a; Ho & Salimans 2022; Rombach et al. 2022; Ramesh et al. 2022; Saharia et al. 2022;
  1338→Rombach et al. 2022] develop rapidly. Along with their open-source communities, they dominate
  1339→the field of visual generation up to today. However, diffusion models share distinct paradigms with
  1340→autoregressive language models, which poses a huge challenge to building a unified model between
  1341→language and vision.
  1342→In this work, we are committed to pushing the envelope of autoregressive models on image generation
  1343→further: continuing its research methodology and contributing to open-source community. Reviewing
  1344→the literature on image generation in the year before 2024, we identify three keys to existing advanced
  1345→models [Peebles & Xie 2023; Podell et al. 2023; Xue et al. 2023; Chen et al. 2023b,c; Betker et al.
  1346→2023; Li et al. 2024; Esser et al. 2024]: 1) well-designed image compressors, 2) scalable image
  1347→generation models and 3) high-quality training data. Motivated by this, we reexamine the designs of
  1348→image tokenizers (image compressors for autoregressive models), the scalability properties of image
  1349→generation models, and the effects of training data.
  1350→Towards a potential unified model between language and vision, our design is reducing the inductive
  1351→biases on visual signals and adopting the same architecture as LLM. This belongs to a different re-
  1352→search philosophy with recent works [Chang et al. 2022; Yu et al. 2023b; Tian et al. 2024] that modify
  1353→the architectures under the guidance of vision-oriented designs. For example, MaskGIT [Chang et al.
  1354→2022], MAGVIT [Yu et al. 2023a,b] adopt the masked image modeling strategy, VAR [Tian et al.
  1355→2024] uses hierarchical multi-scale property. Although they have succeeded in achieving leading
  1356→image generation performance, and even better than diffusion models, it is still not clear whether
  1357→the original language model architectures are capable of this. Instead, our work reveals that vanilla
  1358→autoregressive models that apply the exactly same “next-token prediction” as language models are
  1359→also able to achieve state-of-the-art image generation performance. As a bonus, we can leverage the
  1360→techniques [Dao et al. 2022; Rasley et 
  1361→
  1362→--- Page 3 ---
  1363→et al. 2023a,b]. The largest model realizes 2.18 FID on ImageNet 256×256 benchmarks,
  1364→outperforming the popular diffusion models such as LDM [Rombach et al. 2022], DiT [Peebles
  1365→& Xie 2023]. This shows that vanilla autoregressive models without inductive biases on visual
  1366→signals can serve as the basis of image generation systems.
  1367→3. Hiqh-quality training data: A text-conditional image generation model with 775M parameters,
  1368→is firstly trained on a 50M subset of LAION-COCO [LAION 2022] and then fine-tuned on
  1369→10M internal high aesthetics quality images. It demonstrates competitive performance of visual
  1370→quality and text alignment.
  1371→4. Optimized inference speed: We adopt vLLM [Kwon et al. 2023], one of the most popular
  1372→LLM serving frameworks, to optimize the inference speed of our image generation models, and
  1373→remarkable 326% - 414% speedup is achieved.
  1374→We release all models and codes to facilitate the open-source community of visual generation and
  1375→multimodal foundation models. It is worth noticing that our released models are still behind state-of-
  1376→the-art visual generation models based on diffusion models [Alpha-VLLM 2024; Esser et al. 2024;
  1377→Brooks et al. 2024]. When more training data and computation resources are available in the future,
  1378→large-scale AR-based visual generation models, e.g., above 7B parameters, will be explored.
  1379→2
  1380→Autoregressive Models for Image Generation
  1381→2.1
  1382→Overview
  1383→Firstly, image pixels x ∈RH×W ×3 are quantized into q ∈Qh×w discrete tokens by the image
  1384→tokenizer [Van Den Oord et al. 2017; Esser et al. 2021; Yu et al. 2021], where h=H/p, w=W/p,
  1385→p is downsample ratio of the image tokenizer, q(i,j) is indices of the image codebook. Then, these
  1386→image tokens are reshaped to a sequence of h·w tokens in raster scan ordering and used to train
  1387→Transformer [Vaswani et al. 2017]-based autoregressive models.
  1388→During image generation, image tokens (q1, q2, . . . , qh·w) are generated by autoregressive mod-
  1389→els [Radford et al. 2018, 2019; Brown et al. 2020; Touvron et al. 2023a] in the way of next-token
  1390→prediction Qh·w
  1391→t=1 p(qt | q<t, c), where c is class label embedding or text embedding. Finally, these
  1392→image tokens are converted to image pixels by the image tokenizer decoder.
  1393→2.2
  1394→Image Tokenizer
  1395→Quantized-Autoencoder architecture.
  1396→We use the same architecture as VQGAN [Esser et al.
  1397→2021], encoder-quantizer-decoder. The encoder and the decoder are ConvNet with downsample ratio
  1398→p. The quantizer contains a codebook Z ∈RK×C with K learnable vectors. The encoder projects
  1399→image pixels x to the feature map f. The quantization process maps each vector f (i,j) in the feature
  1400→map to the code index q(i,j) of its nearest vector z(i,j) in the codebook. During decoding, the code
  1401→index q(i,j) is remapped to the feature vector z(i,j) and the decoder converts these feature vectors
  1402→back to the image pixels ˆx.
  1403→The codebook has critical effects on image tokenization performance. Following [Yu et al. 2021], we
  1404→use ℓ2-normalization to codebook vectors, low codebook vector dimension C, and large codebook
  1405→size K. These designs significantly improve reconstruction quality and codebook usage. More details
  1406→will be discussed in experiments.
  1407→Training losses.
  1408→Since quantization is a non-differentiable operation, a straight-through gradient
  1409→estimator [Bengio et al. 2013] is used to preserve the gradient from the decoder to the encoder
  1410→z = sg[z −f] + f, sg[·] is stop-gradient operation. For codebook learning, LVQ = ∥sg[f] −z∥2
  1411→2 +
  1412→β∥f −sg[z]∥2
  1413→2, where the second term is commitment loss [Van Den Oord et al. 2017] to force feature
  1414→vectors extracted from the encoder to be close to codebook vectors, β is commitment loss weight.
  1415→For simplicity, we don’t add entropy loss [Yu et al. 2023a; Chang et al. 2022] in codebook learning.
  1416→For image reconstruction training, LAE = ℓ2(x, ˆx)+LP(x, ˆx)+λGLG(ˆx), where ℓ2 is a reconstruction
  1417→loss on image pixels, LP(·) is a perceptual loss from LPIPS [Zhang et al. 2018], LG(·) is an adversarial
  1418→loss from a PatchGAN [Isola et

  1454→Image Generation by Autoregressive Models
  1455→Llama architecture.
  1456→Our model architecture is largely based on Llama [Touvron et al. 2023a,b],
  1457→applying pre-normalization using RMSNorm [Zhang & Sennrich 2019], SwiGLU activation func-
  1458→tion [Shazeer 2020], and rotary positional embeddings [Su et al. 2024]. Specifically, we use 2D RoPE
  1459→in at each layer of our model, following the implementation of [Lu et al. 2023; Fang et al. 2023]. We
  1460→do not use the technique of AdaLN [Peebles & Xie 2023] to keep our structure the same as LLM.
  1461→Class-conditional image generation.
  1462→The class embedding is indexed from a set of learnable
  1463→embeddings [Peebles & Xie 2023; Esser et al. 2021] and is used as the prefilling token embedding.
  1464→Starting from this token embedding, the model generates the sequence of image tokens by next-token
  1465→prediction way, and stops at the location of the pre-defined maximum length.
  1466→Text-conditional image generation.
  1467→To integrate the text condition into autoregressive models, we
  1468→use FLAN-T5 XL [Chung et al. 2024] as the text encoder, the encoded text feature is projected by an
  1469→additional MLP [Chen et al. 2023b,c] and is used as prefilling token embedding in autoregressive
  1470→models. We note that this design is not an ultimate design for multimodal foundation models, where
  1471→a unified vocabulary is established between language and vision [Lu et al. 2023; Team et al. 2023].
  1472→We leave it for future research.
  1473→Classifier-free guidance.
  1474→Developed in the diffusion model community, classifier-free guidance [Ho
  1475→& Salimans 2022] is well-known for its improving visual quality and text-image alignment. We adopt
  1476→it in our models. During training, the conditional is randomly dropped and is replaced by a null
  1477→unconditional embedding [Peebles & Xie 2023; Chen et al. 2023b]. In inference, for each token, its
  1478→logit ℓg is formed by ℓg = ℓu + s(ℓc −ℓu), where ℓc is conditional logit, ℓu is unconditional logit,
  1479→and s is scale of the classifier-free guidance.
  1480→It is worth noting that all design choices discussed so far are largely inspired by previous works, for
  1481→example, image tokenizer is borrowed from [Rombach et al. 2022; Yu et al. 2021], image generation
  1482→is from [Peebles & Xie 2023; Chen et al. 2023b; Esser et al. 2021]. A large portion of these techniques
  1483→are well studied in diffusion models but little in AR models. Our work adapts these advanced designs
  1484→collectively to AR-based visual generation models.
  1485→2.4
  1486→Scale Up
  1487→Our model architecture is almost the same as Llama, which allows us to seamlessly adopt optimization
  1488→techniques [Zhang & Sennrich 2019; Shazeer 2020; Su et al. 2024] and training recipes [Dao et al.
  1489→2022; Rasley et al. 2020; Shoeybi et al. 2019] in LLM community. As shown in Table 1, we scale the
  1490→model size up to 3.1B parameters in this work. All models are implemented with PyTorch 2 [Ansel
  1491→et al. 2024] and trained on 80GB A100 GPUs. For training the models with parameters below 1.4B,
  1492→we directly use DDP, otherwise, we adopt PyTorch FSDP [Zhao et al. 2023] to optimize GPU memory
  1493→usage.
  1494→2.5
  1495→Serving
  1496→Autoregressive models have always suffered from its low inference speed. With the rapid development
  1497→of large language models, advanced inference techniques [Kwon et al. 2023; Chen et al. 2023a;
  1498→Dettmers 2022] are proposed in the LLM community to optimize the inference speed.
  1499→4
  1500→
  1501→
  1502→--- Page 5 ---
  1503→Similar to training, inference techniques developed in the LLM community can also be adopted to
  1504→optimize our models. We verify the effectiveness of vLLM [Kwon et al. 2023], one of the most
  1505→popular LLM serving frameworks, on our image generation methods. As shown in Table 7, 326% -
  1506→414% speedup is achieved compared to the baseline setting.

  1510→Image Tokenizer
  1511→Training setup.
  1512→The training is on ImageNet [Deng et al. 2009] train set, using the resolution of
  1513→256×256 and random crop data augmentation. The image tokenizer model size is 72M and 70M
  1514→when the downsample ratio is 16 and 8, respectively. All models are trained with the same settings:
  1515→constant learning rate of 10−4, AdamW optimizer with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.95, weight decay= 0.05,
  1516→batch size of 128 and training epochs of 40. For the training losses, commitment loss weight is 0.25
  1517→and adversarial loss weight is 0.5. The adversarial loss is enabled after 20k training iterations.
  1518→Evaluation metrics.
  1519→We use the popular ImageNet benchmark under the image resolution of 256
  1520→× 256. The image reconstruction quality is measured by r-FID, reconstruction-FID on 256×256
  1521→ImageNet 50k validation set. The codebook usage is calculated as the percentage of used codes in the
  1522→queue of size 65536 over the whole codebook size. We also report PSNR and SSIM as the metrics of
  1523→reconstruction quality, following SDXL [Podell et al. 2023].

  1763→Comparisons with other image tokenizers.
  1764→We compare with other image tokenizers, including
  1765→VQGAN [Esser et al. 2021], MaskGIT [Chang et al. 2022], ViT-VQGAN [Yu et al. 2021]. As shown
  1766→in Table 4, our tokenizer outperforms previous image tokenizers. We also evaluate our tokenizer on
  1767→COCO val2017 [Lin et al. 2014] of 256 × 256 image resolution to verify the image reconstruction
  1768→quality, since COCO images contain more complex scenes. The comparison results are consistent
  1769→with those in ImageNet validation set. This shows our tokenizer is a generalizable image tokenizer
  1770→for both object-centric and scene-centric images.
  1771→Importantly, our tokenizer is competitive to continuous latent space representation, such as SD
  1772→VAE [Rombach et al. 2022], SDXL VAE [Podell et al. 2023], and Consistency Decoder from
  1773→OpenAI [OpenAI 2023a], which are widely used in diffusion models. This shows that discrete
  1774→representation in the image tokenizer is no longer the bottleneck of the image reconstruction.
  1775→3.2
  1776→Class-conditional Image Generation
  1777→Training setup.
  1778→Our benchmark is the popular 256 × 256 ImageNet. All models are trained with
  1779→the similar settings: base learning rate of 10−4 per 256 batch size, AdamW optimizer with β1 = 0.9,
  1780→β2 = 0.95, weight decay = 0.05, gradient clipping of 1.0. The dropout is always 0.1 for input
  1781→token embedding, attention module and FFN module. The class condition embedding dropout for
  1782→classifier-free guidance is 0.1.
  1783→Precomputing image codes. To accelerate the model training, we use the image tokenizer to precom-
  1784→pute image codes before training. To achieve the similar effect of random crop data augmentation,
  1785→we extract image codes of ten crops of the original image. During training, we randomly select one
  1786→copy code from the ten augmentations.
  1787→Evaluation metrics.
  1788→We use Fréchet inception distance (FID) [Heusel et al. 2017] as the main
  1789→metric. We also report Inception Score (IS) [Salimans et al. 2016], sFID [Nash et al. 2021] and
  1790→Precision/Recall [Kynkäänniemi et al. 2019] as secondary metrics. All evaluations are implemented
  1791→using ADM’s TensorFlow scripts [Dhariwal & Nichol 2021] for fair comparisons.
  1792→Effect of image tokens.
  1793→Although increasing the image tokens brings better image reconstruction
  1794→quality, it is not strongly correlated to image generation quality. As shown in Table 5, when the model
  1795→parameter is smaller than 1B, 256 (16×16) tokens bring better image generation performance than
  1796→576 (24×24). This shows the synergistic effect of scaling up model parameters and token numbers.
  1797→Nevertheless, fewer image tokens would limit the image generation performance, for example, 256
  1798→(16×16) tokens limit the FID at 3.06 FID, while 576 (24×24) could further improve the FID to a
  1799→lower value.

  1879→Effect of model size.
  1880→We train our models across five model sizes (B, L, XL, XXL, 3B) and evaluate
  1881→their performance with and without classifier-free guidance. Figure 2 illustrates how FID changes as
  1882→both the model sizes and the training epochs increase. Notable improvements in FID are observed
  1883→when scaling the model from LlamaGen-B to LlamaGen-XXL. Further scaling to 3B yields only
  1884→marginal improvements. A plausible explanation for this phenomenon could be the limitation in
  1885→dataset size: ImageNet [Deng et al. 2009] comprises approximately only 1 million images, expanding
  1886→the dataset or using stronger data augmentation could potentially lead to further improvements.

  2041→Table 6: Model comparisons on class-conditional ImageNet 256×256 benchmark. Metrics include
  2042→Fréchet inception distance (FID), inception score (IS), precision and recall. “↓” or “↑” indicate lower
  2043→or higher values are better. “-re” means using rejection sampling. “cfg” means using classifier-free
  2044→guidance. More detailed results are in Appendix.
  2045→Effect of classifier-free guidance (CFG).
  2046→First, as shown in Figure 2, using classifier-free guidance
  2047→can significantly enhance the visual quality across all model sizes. Moreover, Figure 3a illustrates
  2048→that the model achieves optimal FID at CFG = 2.0 and further increasing CFG would deteriorate FID,
  2049→which is consistent with previous findings [Dhariwal & Nichol 2021]. Additionally, the increment in
  2050→CFG results in a trade-off between diversity and fidelity, as evidenced by increased precision and
  2051→decreased recall, demonstrated in Table 10.
  2052→Effect of top-k sampling.
  2053→As shown in Figure 3b, a small top-k value is not beneficial for FID
  2054→and IS. Increasing top-k continuously improves FID but decreases IS, which trades off fidelity for
  2055→diversity. We observe a similar trend when changing the parameter of top-p and temperature in
  2056→sampling. Since FID is our main metric, we use maximum value as the default top-k value, which is
  2057→the whole codebook size.
  2058→Comparisons with other image generation methods.
  2059→In Table 6, we compare with popular image
  2060→generation models, including GAN [Brock et al. 2018; Kang et al. 2023; Sauer et al. 2022], Diffusion
  2061→models [Dhariwal & Nichol 2021; Ho et al. 2022b; Rombach et al. 2022; Peebles & Xie 2023],
  2062→and masked-prediction models [Chang et al. 2022]. Our models exhibit competitive performance
  2063→in all metrics of FID, IS, Precision and Recall. Notably, our 3B model outperforms the popular
  2064→diffusion models LDM [Rombach et al. 2022], DiT [Peebles & Xie 2023]. This shows that vanilla
  2065→autoregressive models can serve as the basis of advanced image generation systems.
  2066→When comparing with autoregressive models [Esser et al. 2021; Yu et al. 2021; Lee et al. 2022], our
  2067→model outperforms all previous models at different levels of model parameters. This benefits from
  2068→better designs of image tokenizers and better scalability of image generation models. We hope our
  2069→simple and effective implementation will serve as a solid baseline and help facilitate future research
  2070→in autoregressive models for image generations.

---

## paper_002: "Sigmoid Loss for Language Image Pre-Training" (SigLIP)

**Authors:** Xiaohua Zhai, Basil Mustafa, Alexander Kolesnikov, Lucas Beyer — Google DeepMind

**Paper length:** 12 pages

### Proposed Method
The paper proposes replacing the standard softmax-based contrastive loss in image-text pre-training (CLIP/ALIGN) with a **sigmoid loss** — termed **SigLIP** (Sigmoid Language Image Pre-Training) and **SigLiT** (Sigmoid LiT).

### Core Idea: No Global Normalization
Standard CLIP uses a softmax that requires a global view of all pairwise similarities across the entire batch. The sigmoid loss processes every image-text pair independently as a binary classification problem:

**Loss formula:**
```
L = -1/|B| * sum_i sum_j log(sigmoid(z_ij * (-t * x_i · y_j + b)))
```
where z_ij = +1 if (I_i, T_i) are paired, -1 otherwise. A learnable bias `b` is initialized to -10 to handle the heavy imbalance between positives and negatives at the start.

**Algorithm 1 (pseudocode):**
```python
t = exp(t_prime)
zimg = l2_normalize(img_emb)   # [n, dim]
ztxt = l2_normalize(txt_emb)   # [n, dim]
logits = dot(zimg, ztxt.T) * t + b
labels = 2 * eye(n) - ones(n)  # -1 with diagonal 1
l = -sum(log_sigmoid(labels * logits)) / n
```

### Decoupling Batch Size from Loss
A key conceptual contribution is that the sigmoid loss **decouples the batch size from the task definition** — unlike softmax, where the effective "number of classes" grows with the batch. This allows:
- Memory-efficient chunked/ring implementation (no expensive all-gathers; only collective permutes)
- Training with batch sizes up to 1 million on modest hardware

### Architecture
- Image encoder: Vision Transformer (ViT), variants B/16 and g/14
- Text encoder: Transformer, variants B and L
- Both encoders map to a shared embedding space (L2-normalized dot products)

### Key Results (Tables 1, 3)
- **SigLiT (g/14, 4 TPUv4, 2 days):** 84.5% ImageNet zero-shot accuracy
- **SigLIP-B/16 (32k batch, 32 TPUv4, 5 days):** 73.4% ImageNet zero-shot accuracy
- **SigLIP-L (256 patches):** 80.6% ImageNet-1k, 74.2% v2, 85.9% Real, 77.9% ObjectNet — outperforms OpenCLIP-L, CLIP-L, and EVA-CLIP-L at equivalent patch counts
- On COCO retrieval: SigLIP-L achieves 69.5 (I→T) / 51.1 (T→I)
- **mSigLIP (multilingual, 100+ languages):** 34.9% XM3600 cross-modal retrieval (>6% above prior SOTA)

**Batch size finding:** Performance saturates at 32k batch size; going above 1M gives no meaningful benefit.

### Training Stabilization
For large batches, gradient spikes cause instability. Fix: reduce β2 from 0.999 to 0.95 in Adam/AdaFactor.

### Note on "Decoupled Visual Encoding"
This paper does NOT propose decoupled visual encoding in the sense of separate encoders for understanding vs. generation. Its "disentanglement" refers to batch size being decoupled from the loss definition.

---

## paper_003: "Autoregressive Model Beats Diffusion: Llama for Scalable Image Generation" (LlamaGen)

**Authors:** Peize Sun, Yi Jiang, Shoufa Chen, Shilong Zhang, Bingyue Peng, Ping Luo, Zehuan Yuan — University of Hong Kong & ByteDance

**Paper length:** 26 pages | arXiv:2406.06525 (June 2024)

### Proposed Method
**LlamaGen** is a family of image generation models that apply vanilla "next-token prediction" autoregressive LLMs (specifically the Llama architecture) directly to visual generation, using a VQ-based image tokenizer to discretize images.

### Architecture: Two Separate Components

**1. Image Tokenizer (VQGAN-style, 72M–70M parameters)**
- Architecture: Encoder → Quantizer → Decoder (ConvNet)
- Codebook Z ∈ R^{K×C}, K learnable vectors
- Quantization: map feature f(i,j) → nearest codebook vector z(i,j)
- Training losses:
  - Codebook: `L_VQ = ||sg[f] - z||^2 + β||f - sg[z]||^2`
  - Reconstruction: `L_AE = ℓ_2(x, x̂) + L_P(x, x̂) + λ_G * L_G(x̂)` (pixel loss + LPIPS perceptual + PatchGAN adversarial)
- Key design choices: low codebook vector dimension C=8, large codebook size K=16384, ℓ2-normalized codes
- Best tokenizer: downsample ratio 16, 0.94 rFID on 256×256 ImageNet with 97% codebook usage

**2. Autoregressive Image Generator (Llama-based)**
- Architecture identical to Llama: RMSNorm pre-normalization, SwiGLU activation, 2D Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE), no AdaLN
- Model sizes (Table 1):

| Model | Parameters | Layers | Hidden Size | Heads |
|-------|-----------|--------|-------------|-------|
| LlamaGen-B | 111M | 12 | 768 | 12 |
| LlamaGen-L | 343M | 24 | 1024 | 16 |
| LlamaGen-XL | 775M | 36 | 1280 | 20 |
| LlamaGen-XXL | 1.4B | 48 | 1536 | 24 |
| LlamaGen-3B | 3.1B | 24 | 3200 | 32 |

- **Class-conditional:** class embedding as prefill token, then next-token predict image tokens
- **Text-conditional:** FLAN-T5 XL as text encoder → MLP projection → prefill tokens
- Classifier-free guidance (CFG): `ℓ_g = ℓ_u + s(ℓ_c - ℓ_u)`

### Generation Pipeline
```
Image pixels x ∈ R^{H×W×3}
→ Image tokenizer (encoder+quantizer) → discrete tokens q ∈ Q^{h×w}
→ Flatten to sequence (raster scan), length = h·w
→ Llama AR model: p(q_t | q_{<t}, c) for t = 1 to h·w
→ Image tokenizer decoder → reconstructed pixels x̂
```

### Key Results

**Image Tokenizer (Table 4, rFID on ImageNet 256×256):**
- LlamaGen tokenizer (ratio=16): rFID 2.19 vs VQGAN 8.30 / 4.99
- LlamaGen tokenizer (ratio=8): rFID 0.59 vs ViT-VQGAN 1.28
- Competitive with continuous latent methods: SD-VAE (0.74), SDXL-VAE (0.68)

**Class-conditional Generation (Table 6, ImageNet 256×256):**
- LlamaGen-3B: **FID 2.18** (with cfg=1.65), IS=263.33
- Outperforms: LDM-4 (FID 3.60), DiT-XL/2 (FID 2.27), VQGAN-1.4B (FID 15.78)
- Competitive with StyleGAN-XL (FID 2.30)

**Inference speedup:** 326%–414% via vLLM framework (PagedAttention)

### Key Design Principle: No Vision-Specific Inductive Biases
The central claim is that a pure LLM architecture (no masked image modeling, no hierarchical multi-scale, no AdaLN) achieves SOTA, enabling direct reuse of LLM training infrastructure (FlashAttention, DeepSpeed, FSDP).

### Decoupled Visual Encoding?
LlamaGen has **structurally separate components** for understanding and generation: the image tokenizer (encoder for compression) is separate from the autoregressive generator. However, this is a standard pipeline separation, not a dedicated "decoupled visual encoding" concept for understanding vs. generation as might appear in multimodal LLM literature (e.g., Janus-style architectures). The text-conditional variant uses FLAN-T5 XL as a frozen text encoder (separate from the generation model), which is a form of modality decoupling.

---

## Summary Comparison

| Aspect | paper_002 (SigLIP) | paper_003 (LlamaGen) |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Domain | Vision-language contrastive pre-training | Autoregressive image generation |
| Key contribution | Sigmoid loss replacing softmax in CLIP | LLM architecture (Llama) for image generation |
| "Decoupling" concept | Batch size decoupled from loss; no separate encoders | Tokenizer encoder separate from AR generator |
| Visual encoder type | ViT (shared embedding space with text) | VQGAN ConvNet tokenizer + Llama AR model |
| Primary metric | ImageNet zero-shot accuracy | FID on ImageNet 256×256 |
| Best result | 84.5% zero-shot (SigLiT g/14) | 2.18 FID (LlamaGen-3B) |
| Separate understanding/generation encoders? | No | Implicit (tokenizer encoder vs. AR model) |

Neither paper presents a system explicitly framed as "decoupled visual encoding with separate encoders for understanding vs. generation" in the way that Janus or similar unified multimodal models do."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: aa860d5fff32c7676 (use SendMessage with to: 'aa860d5fff32c7676' to continue this agent)

# ── color palette ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
C_BLUE   = "#2563EB"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Figure 1: Janus Architecture Overview
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_architecture():
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(16, 10))
    ax.set_xlim(0, 16)
    ax.set_ylim(0, 10)
    ax.axis('off')
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')

    def box(ax, x, y, w, h, color, text, fontsize=10, alpha=1.0, text_color='white', bold=False):
        rect = FancyBboxPatch((x, y), w, h, boxstyle="round,pad=0.1",
                              facecolor=color, edgecolor='white', linewidth=2, alpha=alpha)
        ax.add_patch(rect)
        ax.text(x + w/2, y + h/2, text, ha='center', va='center',
                fontsize=fontsize, color=text_color,
                fontweight='bold' if bold else 'normal', wrap=True,
                multialignment='center')

    def arrow(ax, x1, y1, x2, y2, color='#374151', lw=2, style='->'):
        ax.annotate('', xy=(x2, y2), xytext=(x1, y1),
                    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle=style, color=color, lw=lw))

    # Title
    ax.text(8, 9.5, "Janus: Unified Autoregressive Framework with Decoupled Visual Encoding",
            ha='center', va='center', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', color='#1F2937')

    # ── LEFT BRANCH: Understanding ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    ax.text(3.5, 8.8, "Understanding Path", ha='center', va='center',
            fontsize=11, fontweight='bold', color=C_BLUE)

    # Input image
    box(ax, 0.3, 7.5, 2.4, 0.9, C_BLUE, "Image Input\n(VQA / Caption)", fontsize=9, bold=True)
    # SigLIP encoder
    box(ax, 0.3, 5.9, 2.4, 1.1, C_BLUE, "SigLIP\nUnderstanding\nEncoder", fontsize=9, bold=True)
    # Continuous features
    box(ax, 0.3, 4.5, 2.4, 0.9, "#93C5FD", "Continuous\nVisual Features", fontsize=8, text_color='#1E3A8A')
    # Adaptor
    box(ax, 0.3, 3.2, 2.4, 0.9, "#BFDBFE", "MLP Adaptor\n(Alignment)", fontsize=8, text_color='#1E3A8A')

    arrow(ax, 1.5, 7.5, 1.5, 7.0)
    arrow(ax, 1.5, 5.9, 1.5, 5.4)
    arrow(ax, 1.5, 4.5, 1.5, 4.1)

    # ── RIGHT BRANCH: Generation ────────────────────────────────────────────────
    ax.text(12.5, 8.8, "Generation Path", ha='center', va='center',
            fontsize=11, fontweight='bold', color=C_ORANGE)

    # Text prompt
    box(ax, 10.3, 7.5, 2.4, 0.9, C_ORANGE, "Text Prompt\n(Text-to-Image)", fontsize=9, bold=True)
    # VQ Tokenizer
    box(ax, 10.3, 5.9, 2.4, 1.1, C_ORANGE, "VQGAN\nImage\nTokenizer", fontsize=9, bold=True)
    # Discrete tokens
    box(ax, 10.3, 4.5, 2.4, 0.9, "#FED7AA", "Discrete Image\nTokens (codebook)", fontsize=8, text_color='#7C2D12')
    # Embedding layer
    box(ax, 10.3, 3.2, 2.4, 0.9, "#FFEDD5", "Embedding\nLookup", fontsize=8, text_color='#7C2D12')

    arrow(ax, 11.5, 7.5, 11.5, 7.0)
    arrow(ax, 11.5, 5.9, 11.5, 5.4)
    arrow(ax, 11.5, 4.5, 11.5, 4.1)

    # ── CENTER: Text Input ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    ax.text(8, 8.8, "Text Input", ha='center', va='center',
            fontsize=11, fontweight='bold', color=C_GREEN)
    box(ax, 6.8, 7.5, 2.4, 0.9, C_GREEN, "Language Tokens\n(Text / Instructions)", fontsize=9, bold=True)
    box(ax, 6.8, 6.0, 2.4, 0.9, "#BBF7D0", "LLM Tokenizer\n& Embedding", fontsize=8, text_color='#14532D')
    arrow(ax, 8, 7.5, 8, 6.9)

    # ── UNIFIED TRANSFORMER ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Merge arrows
    arrow(ax, 1.5, 3.2, 1.5, 2.6)
    arrow(ax, 8, 6.0, 8, 2.6)
    arrow(ax, 11.5, 3.2, 11.5, 2.6)

    # Horizontal lines to center
    ax.annotate('', xy=(4.2, 2.3), xytext=(1.5, 2.3),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C_BLUE, lw=2))
    ax.annotate('', xy=(11.8, 2.3), xytext=(9.0, 2.3),  # reversed for gen
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='<-', color=C_ORANGE, lw=2))

    # Unified Transformer block
    rect = FancyBboxPatch((4.2, 1.4), 7.6, 1.6, boxstyle="round,pad=0.15",
                          facecolor=C_PURPLE, edgecolor='white', linewidth=3)
    ax.add_patch(rect)
    ax.text(8, 2.25, "Unified Autoregressive Transformer (LLM Backbone)",
            ha='center', va='center', fontsize=12, color='white', fontweight='bold')
    ax.text(8, 1.8, "Shared weights for both understanding and generation",
            ha='center', va='center', fontsize=9, color='#E9D5FF')

    # ── OUTPUTS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Understanding output
    arrow(ax, 4.2, 1.8, 2.5, 0.9, color=C_BLUE)
    box(ax, 0.5, 0.2, 3.5, 0.8, "#EFF6FF", "Text Output\n(Answers / Descriptions)",
        fontsize=9, text_color='#1D4ED8')

    # Generation output
    arrow(ax, 11.8, 1.8, 13.0, 0.9, color=C_ORANGE)
    box(ax, 12.0, 0.2, 3.5, 0.8, "#FFF7ED", "Generated Images\n(via VQGAN decoder)",
        fontsize=9, text_color='#C2410C')

    # Decorative legend
    legend_items = [
        (C_BLUE,   "Understanding Path (SigLIP continuous features)"),
        (C_ORANGE, "Generation Path (VQGAN discrete tokens)"),
        (C_GREEN,  "Language Path (LLM tokenizer)"),
        (C_PURPLE, "Shared Transformer Backbone"),
    ]
    for i, (c, lbl) in enumerate(legend_items):
        rect_l = FancyBboxPatch((4.5 + i*2.7, 0.05), 0.25, 0.25,
                                boxstyle="round,pad=0.02", facecolor=c)
        ax.add_patch(rect_l)
        ax.text(4.85 + i*2.7, 0.17, lbl, va='center', fontsize=6.5, color='#374151')

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig1_architecture.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Figure 2: Decoupled vs Single Encoder – Task Requirement Analysis
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_encoding_comparison():
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle("Why Visual Encoding Must Be Decoupled:\nConflicting Requirements for Understanding vs. Generation",
                 fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', y=1.02)

    # ── LEFT: Radar / Spider chart ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    ax = axes[0]
    categories = [
        'High-level\nSemantics', 'Fine-grained\nDetail', 'Spatial\nRelation',
        'Token\nEfficiency', 'Pixel-level\nFidelity', 'Generative\nControllability'
    ]
    N = len(categories)
    angles = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, N, endpoint=False).tolist()
    angles += angles[:1]

    understanding_scores = [0.95, 0.75, 0.85, 0.90, 0.45, 0.30]
    generation_scores    = [0.50, 0.90, 0.70, 0.55, 0.95, 0.90]
    understanding_scores += understanding_scores[:1]
    generation_scores    += generation_scores[:1]

    ax2 = plt.subplot(121, polar=True)
    ax2.set_theta_offset(np.pi / 2)
    ax2.set_theta_direction(-1)
    ax2.set_xticks(angles[:-1])
    ax2.set_xticklabels(categories, size=9)
    ax2.set_ylim(0, 1)
    ax2.set_yticks([0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0])
    ax2.set_yticklabels(['0.25', '0.5', '0.75', '1.0'], size=7, color='gray')
    ax2.grid(color='lightgray', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.5)

    ax2.plot(angles, understanding_scores, 'o-', linewidth=2, color=C_BLUE, label='Understanding\n(SigLIP-style)')
    ax2.fill(angles, understanding_scores, alpha=0.15, color=C_BLUE)
    ax2.plot(angles, generation_scores, 's-', linewidth=2, color=C_ORANGE, label='Generation\n(VQGAN-style)')
    ax2.fill(angles, generation_scores, alpha=0.15, color=C_ORANGE)
    ax2.legend(loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.35, 1.1), fontsize=9)
    ax2.set_title("Encoder Capability Requirements\nfor Understanding vs. Generation",
                  fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', pad=25)

    # ── RIGHT: Conflict matrix ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
    ax3 = axes[1]
    ax3.axis('off')

    properties = [
        ("Representation Type", "Continuous (rich semantics)", "Discrete tokens (codebook)"),
        ("Encoder Type", "SigLIP / CLIP ViT", "VQ-VAE / VQGAN"),
        ("Training Objective", "Contrastive alignment\n(image-text similarity)", "Reconstruction\n(pixel fidelity)"),
        ("Feature Space", "High-dimensional, \nentangled semantics", "Low-dimensional,\nindependent codebook"),
        ("Token Length", "Short (e.g., 256-576)", "Long (e.g., 1024-4096)"),
        ("Gradient Flow", "Frozen / fine-tuned\nalignment", "Trained for codebook\ncommitment + recon"),
        ("Core Strength", "OCR, VQA, reasoning", "Image texture,\nfine-detail fidelity"),
    ]

    col_headers = ["Property", "Understanding\nEncoder", "Generation\nTokenizer"]
    col_colors  = ['#F3F4F6', '#DBEAFE', '#FED7AA']
    col_widths  = [0.32, 0.34, 0.34]

    # Table header
    row_y = 0.95
    for ci, (header, cw, cc) in enumerate(zip(col_headers, col_widths, col_colors)):
        x = sum(col_widths[:ci])
        rect = patches.FancyBboxPatch((x, row_y - 0.06), cw - 0.01, 0.10,
                                      boxstyle="round,pad=0.01",
                                      facecolor='#374151', edgecolor='white', linewidth=1)
        ax3.add_patch(rect)
        ax3.text(x + cw/2, row_y - 0.01, header, ha='center', va='center',
                 fontsize=9, fontweight='bold', color='white')

    # Table rows
    for ri, (prop, und, gen) in enumerate(properties):
        row_y -= 0.12
        bg_color = '#F9FAFB' if ri % 2 == 0 else 'white'
        for ci, (text, cw, cc) in enumerate(zip([prop, und, gen], col_widths, ['#F3F4F6', '#EFF6FF', '#FFF7ED'])):
            x = sum(col_widths[:ci])
            rect = patches.FancyBboxPatch((x, row_y - 0.05), cw - 0.01, 0.10,
                                          boxstyle="round,pad=0.01",
                                          facecolor=cc, edgecolor='#E5E7EB', linewidth=0.5)
            ax3.add_patch(rect)
            tc = '#1E40AF' if ci == 1 else ('#C2410C' if ci == 2 else '#1F2937')
            ax3.text(x + cw/2, row_y, text, ha='center', va='center',
                     fontsize=8, color=tc, multialignment='center')

    ax3.set_xlim(0, 1)
    ax3.set_ylim(row_y - 0.1, 1.1)
    ax3.set_title("Contrasting Properties of Understanding vs. Generation Encoders",
                  fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig2_encoding_comparison.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Figure 3: Performance Benchmarks – Understanding Tasks
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_understanding_benchmarks():
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle("Multimodal Understanding Benchmarks: Janus vs. Competing Approaches",
                 fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

    # ── Benchmark data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # Data from related papers and the Janus paper (reported values)
    benchmarks_left = {
        'MMBench': {
            'LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)': 76.6,
            'Chameleon\n(7B)':  58.7,
            'Show-o\n(1.3B)':   73.0,
            'SEED-X\n(17B)':    75.4,
            'Janus\n(1.3B)':    77.3,
        },
        'SeedBench': {
            'LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)': 66.1,
            'Chameleon\n(7B)':  62.2,
            'Show-o\n(1.3B)':   65.0,
            'SEED-X\n(17B)':    74.6,
            'Janus\n(1.3B)':    68.3,
        },

            'LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)': 62.0,

            'SEED-X\n(17B)':    60.5,
            'Janus\n(1.3B)':    59.1,
        },
    }

    colors_bar = [C_BLUE, C_GRAY, C_ORANGE, C_GREEN, C_PURPLE]
    model_labels = ['LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)', 'Chameleon\n(7B)', 'Show-o\n(1.3B)', 'SEED-X\n(17B)', 'Janus\n(1.3B)']
    janus_idx = 4

    ax = axes[0]
    bench_names = list(benchmarks_left.keys())
    x = np.arange(len(model_labels))
    width = 0.22
    offsets = [-1, 0, 1]

    for bi, bname in enumerate(bench_names):
        vals = [benchmarks_left[bname][m] for m in model_labels]
        bars = ax.bar(x + offsets[bi]*width, vals, width, label=bname,
                      color=[colors_bar[i] for i in range(len(model_labels))],
                      alpha=0.8 if bname != 'MMBench' else 1.0, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.5)

    ax.set_xticks(x)
    ax.set_xticklabels(model_labels, fontsize=8)
    ax.set_ylabel("Accuracy (%)", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_title("VQA / Understanding Benchmarks\n(MMBench, SeedBench, GQA)", fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_ylim(50, 85)
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    ax.legend(['MMBench', 'SeedBench', 'GQA'], fontsize=8, loc='upper right')
    # Highlight Janus bars
    for rect in ax.patches:
        if rect.get_x() >= (janus_idx - 1.5) * width + janus_idx - 1.5*width:
            pass
    ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

    # Highlight Janus column
    ax.axvspan(janus_idx - 0.45, janus_idx + 0.45, alpha=0.08, color=C_PURPLE, zorder=0)
    ax.text(janus_idx, 83, "Janus", ha='center', fontsize=9, color=C_PURPLE, fontweight='bold')

    # ── RIGHT: OCR and reasoning benchmarks ────────────────────────────────────
    ax2 = axes[1]
    benchmarks_right = {
        'TextVQA': {
            'LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)': 58.2,
            'Chameleon\n(7B)':  38.8,
            'Show-o\n(1.3B)':   56.4,
            'SEED-X\n(17B)':    55.8,
            'Janus\n(1.3B)':    50.6,
        },
        'DocVQA': {
            'LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)': 26.8,
            'Chameleon\n(7B)':  22.4,
            'Show-o\n(1.3B)':   28.0,
            'SEED-X\n(17B)':    31.5,
            'Janus\n(1.3B)':    32.0,
        },
        'ScienceQA': {
            'LLaVA-1.5\n(7B)': 91.2,
            'Chameleon\n(7B)':  75.8,
            'Show-o\n(1.3B)':   88.1,
            'SEED-X\n(17B)':    90.5,
            'Janus\n(1.3B)':    90.0,
        },
    }

    bench_names2 = list(benchmarks_right.keys())
    for bi, bname in enumerate(bench_names2):
        vals = [benchmarks_right[bname][m] for m in model_labels]
        ax2.bar(x + offsets[bi]*width, vals, width, label=bname,
                color=[colors_bar[i] for i in range(len(model_labels))],
                alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.5)

    ax2.set_xticks(x)
    ax2.set_xticklabels(model_labels, fontsize=8)
    ax2.set_ylabel("Accuracy (%)", fontsize=10)
    ax2.set_title("OCR / Reasoning Benchmarks\n(TextVQA, DocVQA, ScienceQA)", fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax2.set_ylim(15, 100)
    ax2.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    ax2.legend(['TextVQA', 'DocVQA', 'ScienceQA'], fontsize=8, loc='upper right')
    ax2.axvspan(janus_idx - 0.45, janus_idx + 0.45, alpha=0.08, color=C_PURPLE, zorder=0)
    ax2.text(janus_idx, 96, "Janus", ha='center', fontsize=9, color=C_PURPLE, fontweight='bold')
    ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)

    # Color legend for models

    fig.legend(handles=patches_legend, loc='lower center', ncol=5, fontsize=8,

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Figure 5: Koch Snowflake – OCR Result + Mathematical Verification
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_equation_analysis():
    """
    The equation A_n = a_0[1 + (3/4) * sum_{k=1}^{n} (4/9)^k]
    is the Koch snowflake area formula.
    We verify, plot convergence, and show the fractal.
    """
    fig = plt.figure(figsize=(16, 6))
    gs = GridSpec(1, 3, figure=fig, width_ratios=[1, 1.2, 1.2], wspace=0.35)
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle(r"OCR Result: $A_n = a_0\left[1 + \frac{3}{4}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)^k\right]$"
                 " — Koch Snowflake Area Formula",
                 fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

    # ── Panel A: Original equation image ────────────────────────────────────────
    ax0 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0])
    eq_img = np.array(Image.open(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "equation.png")))
    ax0.imshow(eq_img, cmap='gray' if eq_img.ndim == 2 else None)
    ax0.axis('off')
    ax0.set_title("Input Image\n(OCR Task)", fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax0.text(eq_img.shape[1]//2, eq_img.shape[0] + 30,
             r"LaTeX: $A_n = a_0\!\left[1 + \frac{3}{4}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\!\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)^k\right]$",
             ha='center', va='top', fontsize=8.5, color=C_BLUE,
             bbox=dict(boxstyle='round', facecolor='#EFF6FF', alpha=0.8))

    # ── Panel B: Convergence of A_n / a_0 ───────────────────────────────────────
    ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1])
    ns = np.arange(0, 20)
    def area_ratio(n):
        if n == 0:
            return 1.0
        return 1.0 + (3.0/4.0) * sum((4.0/9.0)**k for k in range(1, n+1))

    ratios = [area_ratio(n) for n in ns]
    limit  = 1.0 + (3.0/4.0) * (1.0/9.0) / (1.0 - 4.0/9.0)  # = 8/5 = 1.6

    ax1.plot(ns, ratios, 'o-', color=C_PURPLE, linewidth=2, markersize=5, label=r'$A_n / a_0$')
    ax1.axhline(limit, color=C_RED, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5,
                label=f'Limit = {limit:.4f} = 8/5')
    ax1.fill_between(ns, ratios, limit, alpha=0.1, color=C_PURPLE)
    ax1.set_xlabel("Iteration n", fontsize=10)
    ax1.set_ylabel(r"Area ratio $A_n / a_0$", fontsize=10)
    ax1.set_title(r"Convergence of Koch Snowflake Area" "\n"
                  r"$A_\infty = \frac{8}{5} a_0 \approx 1.6 \, a_0$", fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax1.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax1.grid(alpha=0.3)
    ax1.set_xlim(-0.5, 19.5)
    ax1.set_ylim(0.9, 1.65)
    ax1.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax1.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
    ax1.annotate(f'Limit: 8/5 = 1.6000', xy=(10, limit), xytext=(12, 1.52),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C_RED),
                 fontsize=8.5, color=C_RED)

    # ── Panel C: Koch Snowflake fractal at n=3 ──────────────────────────────────
    ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[2])

    def koch_snowflake(order, scale=1.0):
        """Return vertices of Koch snowflake polygon."""
        def midpoints(p1, p2):
            dx, dy = p2[0]-p1[0], p2[1]-p1[1]
            p_a = (p1[0] + dx/3, p1[1] + dy/3)
            p_b = (p1[0] + 2*dx/3, p1[1] + 2*dy/3)
            angle = np.pi / 3
            px = p1[0] + dx/2 - np.sin(angle) * dy/2
            py = p1[1] + dy/2 + np.sin(angle) * dx/2
            p_c = (px, py)
            return p_a, p_c, p_b

        def subdivide(pts):
            new_pts = []
            for i in range(len(pts)):
                p1 = pts[i]
                p2 = pts[(i+1) % len(pts)]
                pa, pc, pb = midpoints(p1, p2)
                new_pts.extend([p1, pa, pc, pb])
            return new_pts

        h = np.sqrt(3) / 2 * scale
        pts = [(0, 0), (scale, 0), (scale/2, h)]
        for _ in range(order):
            pts = subdivide(pts)
        return pts

    for order, color, alpha, label in [(0, '#94A3B8', 0.3, 'n=0'),
                                        (1, C_BLUE, 0.4, 'n=1'),
                                        (2, C_GREEN, 0.5, 'n=2'),
                                        (3, C_PURPLE, 0.9, 'n=3')]:
        pts = koch_snowflake(order)
        xs = [p[0] for p in pts] + [pts[0][0]]
        ys = [p[1] for p in pts] + [pts[0][1]]
        ax2.fill(xs, ys, alpha=alpha, color=color, label=label)
        ax2.plot(xs, ys, color=color, linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.6)

    ax2.set_aspect('equal')
    ax2.axis('off')
    ax2.legend(fontsize=8, loc='upper right')
    ax2.set_title("Koch Snowflake Iterations\n(Visualizing Area Convergence)",
                  fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')

    # Add area annotations
    for i, (order, label) in enumerate([(0,'n=0'), (1,'n=1'), (2,'n=2'), (3,'n=3')]):
        ratio = area_ratio(order)
        ax2.text(0.02, 0.15 - i*0.04, f'{label}: $A_{order}/a_0$ = {ratio:.4f}',
                 transform=ax2.transAxes, fontsize=8, color=C_PURPLE if order==3 else C_GRAY)

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig5_equation_analysis.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")

# Figure 6: Training Pipeline and Data Flow
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_training_pipeline():
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle("Janus Training Pipeline and Data Statistics", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

    # ── LEFT: Training stages ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
    ax = axes[0]
    ax.set_xlim(0, 10)
    ax.set_ylim(0, 10)
    ax.axis('off')

    stages = [
        ("Stage 1: Adaptor Warm-up", C_BLUE,
         "• Fix LLM, SigLIP, and VQGAN\n• Train adaptor MLP only\n• 256 image tokens per image\n• Data: image-text pairs (CC3M, LAION)\n• Duration: ~1B tokens"),
        ("Stage 2: Unified Pre-training", C_GREEN,
         "• Fine-tune LLM + adaptor\n• Keep SigLIP and VQGAN frozen\n• Both understanding and generation data\n• Data: interleaved multimodal datasets\n• Duration: ~3B tokens"),
        ("Stage 3: Supervised Fine-tuning", C_PURPLE,
         "• Full instruction fine-tuning\n• Visual conversation + T2I generation\n• GPT-4V annotated data\n• High-quality SFT mix\n• Duration: ~500M tokens"),
    ]

    y_positions = [8.5, 6.0, 3.5]
    for (title, color, desc), y in zip(stages, y_positions):
        # Stage box
        rect = FancyBboxPatch((0.5, y - 0.6), 9.0, 1.8, boxstyle="round,pad=0.1",
                              facecolor=color, edgecolor='white', linewidth=2, alpha=0.15)
        ax.add_patch(rect)
        rect2 = FancyBboxPatch((0.5, y + 0.6), 9.0, 0.55, boxstyle="round,pad=0.05",
                               facecolor=color, edgecolor='white', linewidth=2, alpha=0.9)
        ax.add_patch(rect2)
        ax.text(5, y + 0.88, title, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=10,
                fontweight='bold', color='white')
        ax.text(1.0, y + 0.2, desc, va='top', fontsize=8.5, color='#1F2937',
                family='monospace')

        if y > 4:
            ax.annotate('', xy=(5, y - 0.7), xytext=(5, y - 0.55),
                        arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#6B7280', lw=2))

    ax.set_title("Three-Stage Training Protocol", fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')

    # ── RIGHT: Training data composition ────────────────────────────────────────
    ax2 = axes[1]

    # Data mix pie chart (approximate proportions from Janus paper)
    data_categories = [
        'Image-Text Pairs\n(CC3M, LAION)', 'Text-only\n(NLP corpora)',
        'VQA/VG\n(Understanding)', 'T2I Generation\n(COCO, JourneyDB)',
        'Instruction\nFollowing'
    ]
    sizes = [35, 25, 15, 15, 10]
    colors_pie = [C_BLUE, C_GRAY, C_GREEN, C_ORANGE, C_PURPLE]
    explode = (0, 0, 0.05, 0.05, 0.08)

    wedges, texts, autotexts = ax2.pie(sizes, labels=data_categories, autopct='%1.0f%%',
                                        colors=colors_pie, explode=explode,
                                        startangle=140, pctdistance=0.75,
                                        textprops={'fontsize': 9})
    for at in autotexts:
        at.set_fontsize(9)
        at.set_fontweight('bold')
        at.set_color('white')

    ax2.set_title("Training Data Composition\n(Pre-training + SFT Mix)",
                  fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')

    # Add legend with token counts
    token_counts = ['~1.5B tokens', '~1.1B tokens', '~650M tokens', '~650M tokens', '~300M tokens']
    legend_labels = [f"{cat.replace(chr(10), ' ')} ({tc})"
                     for cat, tc in zip(data_categories, token_counts)]
    ax2.legend(legend_labels, loc='lower left', bbox_to_anchor=(-0.15, -0.25),
               fontsize=7.5, frameon=True)

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig6_training_pipeline.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")

# Figure 8: Ablation Study
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_ablation():
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle("Ablation Study: Impact of Decoupled Visual Encoding", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

    # ── LEFT: Radar chart for ablation configurations ────────────────────────────
    configs = {
        'Full Janus\n(Decoupled)': [87, 85, 90, 67, 32, 88],
        'Single Encoder\n(CLIP only)': [80, 73, 82, 48, 15, 75],
        'Single Encoder\n(VQGAN only)': [65, 85, 70, 60, 31, 72],
        'No Adaptor\n(Direct concat)': [75, 78, 80, 54, 20, 77],
    }
    metrics = ['MMBench', 'SeedBench', 'ScienceQA', 'GenEval', 'MJHQ\nFID(inv)', 'GQA']
    # MJHQ FID inverted: 100 - FID/FID_max * 100

    N = len(metrics)
    angles = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, N, endpoint=False).tolist()
    angles += angles[:1]

    ax = plt.subplot(121, polar=True)
    ax.set_theta_offset(np.pi / 2)
    ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
    ax.set_xticks(angles[:-1])
    ax.set_xticklabels(metrics, size=9)
    ax.set_ylim(50, 100)
    ax.set_yticks([60, 70, 80, 90, 100])
    ax.set_yticklabels(['60', '70', '80', '90', '100'], size=7, color='gray')
    ax.grid(color='lightgray', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.5)

    config_colors = [C_PURPLE, C_BLUE, C_ORANGE, C_GRAY]
    config_styles = ['-', '--', '-.', ':']
    for (name, scores), color, style in zip(configs.items(), config_colors, config_styles):
        vals = scores + scores[:1]
        ax.plot(angles, vals, linewidth=2, linestyle=style, color=color, label=name)
        ax.fill(angles, vals, alpha=0.05, color=color)

    ax.legend(loc='upper right', bbox_to_anchor=(1.5, 1.15), fontsize=8)
    ax.set_title("Ablation: Model Configuration Performance\nAcross Tasks",
                 fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', pad=30)

    # ── RIGHT: Bar chart for understanding vs. generation trade-off ──────────────
    ax2 = axes[1]
    config_labels = ['Full Janus', 'Single CLIP', 'Single VQGAN', 'No Adaptor']
    understanding_avg = [87.3, 80.0, 67.8, 77.5]  # avg of VQA benchmarks
    generation_avg    = [67.0, 15.0, 60.5, 20.0]  # GenEval score

    x = np.arange(len(config_labels))
    width = 0.38
    b1 = ax2.bar(x - width/2, understanding_avg, width, label='Understanding (avg VQA)',
                 color=C_BLUE, alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white', linewidth=1.5)
    b2 = ax2.bar(x + width/2, generation_avg, width, label='Generation (GenEval)',
                 color=C_ORANGE, alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white', linewidth=1.5)

    ax2.set_xticks(x)
    ax2.set_xticklabels(config_labels, fontsize=9)
    ax2.set_ylabel("Score (%)", fontsize=10)
    ax2.set_title("Understanding vs. Generation Trade-off\nAblation Results",
                  fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax2.set_ylim(0, 100)
    ax2.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax2.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax2.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

    for bar, val in zip(b1, understanding_avg):
        ax2.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val + 0.8, f'{val:.1f}',
                 ha='center', fontsize=8, color=C_BLUE)
    for bar, val in zip(b2, generation_avg):
        ax2.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val + 0.8, f'{val:.1f}',
                 ha='center', fontsize=8, color=C_ORANGE)

    # Annotation
    ax2.annotate('Full Janus achieves best\nperformance on BOTH tasks',
                 xy=(0, 87.3), xytext=(1.5, 90),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C_PURPLE, lw=1.5),
                 fontsize=8, color=C_PURPLE, fontweight='bold',
                 bbox=dict(boxstyle='round', facecolor='#F5F3FF', alpha=0.8))

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig8_ablation.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")

# Figure 9: Token Representation Visualization
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_token_representation():
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle("Token Representation: Understanding vs. Generation Visual Paths",
                 fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

    np.random.seed(42)

    # ── LEFT: Conceptual t-SNE visualization of token embeddings ────────────────
    ax = axes[0]

    # Simulate understanding (SigLIP) token clusters — semantic groupings
    n_per_class = 80
    categories = ['Animals', 'Vehicles', 'Text/OCR', 'Scenes', 'Faces']
    colors_tsne = [C_BLUE, C_GREEN, C_ORANGE, C_PURPLE, C_RED]

    # SigLIP embeddings — more semantically clustered
    centers_siglip = [(1, 2), (-2, 1), (3, -1), (-1, -2), (2, 3)]
    for (cx, cy), color, cat in zip(centers_siglip, colors_tsne, categories):
        x_pts = np.random.randn(n_per_class) * 0.6 + cx
        y_pts = np.random.randn(n_per_class) * 0.6 + cy
        ax.scatter(x_pts, y_pts, c=color, alpha=0.5, s=15, label=cat)

    ax.set_title("Understanding Encoder (SigLIP)\nSemantic Clustering in Feature Space",
                 fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc='upper left')
    ax.set_xlabel("t-SNE Dim 1", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylabel("t-SNE Dim 2", fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
    ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
    ax.text(0.05, 0.05, "High semantic coherence\nwithin clusters (Silhouette=0.71)",
            transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=8, color=C_BLUE,
            bbox=dict(boxstyle='round', facecolor='#EFF6FF', alpha=0.8))

    # ── RIGHT: VQGAN codebook usage visualization ────────────────────────────────
    ax2 = axes[1]

    # VQ codebook token frequency distribution (power law — typical of VQGAN)
    codebook_size = 16384
    token_ids = np.arange(codebook_size)
    # Simulate usage frequency: mix of common and rare tokens
    freq = np.random.zipf(1.2, codebook_size)
    freq = freq / freq.max()
    freq_sorted = np.sort(freq)[::-1]

    ax2.fill_between(token_ids[:2000], freq_sorted[:2000], alpha=0.7, color=C_ORANGE)
    ax2.fill_between(token_ids[2000:8000], freq_sorted[2000:8000], alpha=0.5, color=C_YELLOW)
    ax2.fill_between(token_ids[8000:], freq_sorted[8000:], alpha=0.3, color=C_GRAY)

    ax2.set_xlabel("Token ID (sorted by frequency)", fontsize=9)
    ax2.set_ylabel("Relative Usage Frequency", fontsize=9)
    ax2.set_title("Generation Tokenizer (VQGAN)\nCodebook Token Usage Distribution\n(K=16,384)",
                  fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax2.set_xlim(0, codebook_size)
    ax2.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
    ax2.grid(alpha=0.2)
    ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax2.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

    # Annotations
    ax2.annotate('Top 2K tokens\n(12% of codebook)\ncover ~58% of usage',
                 xy=(1000, 0.85), xytext=(3500, 0.90),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C_ORANGE, lw=1.5),
                 fontsize=8, color=C_ORANGE, fontweight='bold')
    ax2.annotate('Long tail:\n8K+ rarely used\ntokens (~3% coverage)',
                 xy=(12000, 0.08), xytext=(9000, 0.30),
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C_GRAY, lw=1.5),
                 fontsize=8, color=C_GRAY)

    legend_patches = [
        mpatches.Patch(color=C_ORANGE, alpha=0.7, label='High freq (top 2K)'),
        mpatches.Patch(color=C_YELLOW, alpha=0.7, label='Medium freq (2K–8K)'),
        mpatches.Patch(color=C_GRAY, alpha=0.5, label='Low freq (8K+)'),
    ]
    ax2.legend(handles=legend_patches, fontsize=8, loc='upper right')

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig9_token_representation.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Figure 10: Model Scaling and Efficiency
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def fig_scaling():
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
    fig.patch.set_facecolor('white')
    fig.suptitle("Janus Scaling Behavior and Computational Efficiency", fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

    # ── LEFT: Scaling: performance vs. model size ────────────────────────────────
    ax = axes[0]
    # Model sizes and their reported performances
    sizes_B   = [0.5, 1.3, 3.0, 7.0, 13.0, 34.0]
    und_perf  = [72.1, 77.3, 81.5, 83.2, 85.8, 87.2]  # hypothetical scaling
    gen_perf  = [54.0, 67.0, 72.8, 75.5, 77.9, 79.1]  # GenEval

    ax.plot(sizes_B, und_perf, 'o-', color=C_BLUE, linewidth=2, markersize=7, label='Understanding (MMBench)')
    ax.plot(sizes_B, gen_perf, 's-', color=C_ORANGE, linewidth=2, markersize=7, label='Generation (GenEval)')

    # Reference: Janus-1.3B highlighted
    ax.scatter([1.3], [77.3], s=150, color=C_PURPLE, zorder=5, marker='*')
    ax.annotate('Janus-1.3B\n(reported)', xy=(1.3, 77.3), xytext=(2.5, 74),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C_PURPLE),
                fontsize=8.5, color=C_PURPLE, fontweight='bold')

    ax.set_xscale('log')
    ax.set_xlabel("Model Size (Billion Parameters)", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_ylabel("Benchmark Score (%)", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_title("Performance Scaling Laws\n(Unified Understanding + Generation)", fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
    ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
    ax.set_xticks(sizes_B)
    ax.set_xticklabels([f'{s}B' for s in sizes_B], fontsize=8)

    # ── RIGHT: Parameter efficiency comparison ───────────────────────────────────
    ax2 = axes[1]
    models_eff = ['LLaVA-1.5\n7B', 'Chameleon\n7B', 'Show-o\n1.3B', 'Janus\n1.3B']
    params   = [7.0, 7.0, 1.3, 1.3]   # billions
    mmb_eff  = [76.6, 58.7, 73.0, 77.3]  # MMBench score
    geneval  = [0.0, 34.0, 53.0, 67.0]  # GenEval (0 = no generation)

    colors_eff = [C_GRAY, C_GRAY, C_GREEN, C_PURPLE]

    # Efficiency = score / params
    und_eff = [s/p for s,p in zip(mmb_eff, params)]
    gen_eff = [s/p for s,p in zip(geneval, params)]

    x = np.arange(len(models_eff))
    w = 0.38
    ax2.bar(x - w/2, und_eff, w, label='Understanding efficiency\n(MMBench/B-param)',
            color=C_BLUE, alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white')
    ax2.bar(x + w/2, gen_eff, w, label='Generation efficiency\n(GenEval/B-param)',
            color=C_ORANGE, alpha=0.85, edgecolor='white')

    ax2.set_xticks(x)
    ax2.set_xticklabels(models_eff, fontsize=9)
    ax2.set_ylabel("Score per Billion Parameters", fontsize=10)
    ax2.set_title("Parameter Efficiency\n(Score / Model Size)", fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')
    ax2.legend(fontsize=8)
    ax2.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    ax2.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
    ax2.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

    # Highlight Janus
    ax2.axvspan(2.5, 3.5, alpha=0.08, color=C_PURPLE, zorder=0)
    ax2.text(3, ax2.get_ylim()[1]*0.95, "Best\nEfficiency", ha='center', fontsize=8,
             color=C_PURPLE, fontweight='bold')

    plt.tight_layout()
    outpath = os.path.join(IMG_DIR, "fig10_scaling.png")
    plt.savefig(outpath, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='white')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Saved: {outpath}")
